questions.explanation: The passage describes the time-inconsistency problem as officials facing 'incentives to pursue expansionary monetary policies before elections to boost employment, even at the cost of higher inflation.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to critics mentioned in the passage, quantitative easing is controversial partly because it

options.text: has been proven to cause hyperinflation in all countries that have used it
may disproportionately benefit wealthy households while providing less help to wage earners
violates international trade agreements signed by central banks
requires approval from elected officials before implementation
has no effect on financial markets or the real economy

questions.explanation: The passage states critics argue that 'asset purchases disproportionately benefit wealthy households who own financial assets, while doing little for wage earners and the unemployed.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author's tone regarding proposed reforms to central bank governance can best be described as

options.text: dismissive, viewing reforms as unnecessary and destabilizing
balanced, recognizing the difficulty of preserving independence while enhancing accountability
enthusiastic, strongly endorsing all proposed reforms
cynical, suggesting that no reform can address the fundamental problems
confused, unable to identify which reforms would be most effective

questions.explanation: The passage describes reform proposals neutrally and concludes by framing the issue as a 'challenge' of balancing independence and accountability, indicating a balanced assessment. This matches B.

passage: The interpretation of quantum mechanics remains deeply contested more than a century after the theory's mathematical framework was established. The Copenhagen interpretation, long considered the orthodox view, holds that quantum systems do not possess definite properties until they are measured. Before measurement, a particle exists in a superposition of all possible states, described by a wave function that encodes the probabilities of each outcome. The act of measurement causes the wave function to 'collapse' into a single definite state. This interpretation troubled Einstein, who famously objected that God does not play dice, and it continues to generate philosophical discomfort because it appears to assign a special role to the observer without clearly defining what constitutes an observation. The many-worlds interpretation, proposed by Hugh Everett in 1957, offers a radically different picture. According to Everett, the wave function never collapses; instead, every possible outcome of a quantum measurement is realized in a branching universe. When a physicist measures a particle that could be spin-up or spin-down, both outcomes occur, but in separate branches of reality that thereafter evolve independently. Proponents argue that this interpretation is more parsimonious in its physical assumptions, as it requires no special collapse mechanism, but critics object that it is extravagant in its ontological commitments, positing an effectively infinite number of unobservable universes. More recently, relational quantum mechanics has proposed that the properties of quantum systems are not absolute but are defined only relative to the observer interacting with the system, a view that draws on insights from both earlier interpretations while avoiding some of their difficulties. The persistence of this foundational debate suggests that quantum mechanics, despite its extraordinary predictive success, has yet to yield a fully satisfying account of the nature of physical reality.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: providing a mathematical proof of quantum mechanical principles
presenting competing interpretations of quantum mechanics and their respective strengths and weaknesses
arguing that the many-worlds interpretation is definitively correct
explaining how quantum computers work
describing Einstein's contributions to quantum physics

questions.explanation: The passage presents three interpretations (Copenhagen, many-worlds, relational) with their respective arguments and criticisms. It does not provide mathematical proofs (A), endorse one interpretation (C), discuss quantum computing (D), or focus on Einstein's contributions (E).

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, the many-worlds interpretation is considered parsimonious in its physical assumptions because it

options.text: requires fewer mathematical equations than the Copenhagen interpretation
does not require a special wave function collapse mechanism
posits only a single universe in which all events occur
has been experimentally verified more thoroughly than other interpretations
eliminates the need for quantum superposition as a concept

questions.explanation: The passage states the many-worlds interpretation 'requires no special collapse mechanism,' which is why proponents consider it more parsimonious in physical assumptions. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author's reference to Einstein's objection primarily serves to

options.text: prove that the Copenhagen interpretation is incorrect
illustrate the longstanding philosophical discomfort with the Copenhagen interpretation's implications
argue that Einstein was the most important figure in quantum physics
suggest that religious beliefs should influence scientific interpretation
demonstrate that Einstein supported the many-worlds interpretation

questions.explanation: Einstein's objection is mentioned to illustrate philosophical discomfort with the Copenhagen interpretation, which 'continues to generate philosophical discomfort.' This matches B.

passage: The debate surrounding the role of industrial policy in economic development has intensified as nations grapple with the challenges of technological competition and climate transition. Industrial policy, broadly defined as government intervention aimed at promoting specific sectors or technologies, fell out of favor among mainstream economists during the 1980s and 1990s, when the prevailing consensus held that free markets allocate resources more efficiently than government planners. The Washington Consensus, as this set of policy prescriptions came to be known, advocated privatization, deregulation, and trade liberalization as the path to development. However, the success of East Asian economies, particularly South Korea, Taiwan, and Singapore, which deployed aggressive industrial policies to transform themselves from low-income to high-income economies within a generation, posed a persistent challenge to this orthodoxy. More recently, China's state-directed investment in advanced manufacturing, artificial intelligence, and green energy technologies has compelled Western policymakers to reconsider their skepticism toward industrial strategy. The passage of the CHIPS Act and the Inflation Reduction Act in the United States represented a marked departure from the laissez-faire approach, committing hundreds of billions of dollars to domestic semiconductor manufacturing and clean energy development. Critics of the new industrial policy warn that governments are poorly equipped to pick technological winners, that subsidies invite rent-seeking and corruption, and that retaliatory industrial policies could fragment the global trading system. Proponents counter that market failures in areas such as climate change and national security justify strategic government intervention, and that the question is not whether to pursue industrial policy but how to design institutions capable of implementing it effectively.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: proving that the Washington Consensus was entirely wrong about economic development
tracing the evolving debate over industrial policy and the factors driving its resurgence
comparing the economic growth rates of East Asian and Western economies
explaining the technical provisions of the CHIPS Act in detail
arguing that all government intervention in the economy is harmful

questions.explanation: The passage traces the debate from the Washington Consensus through East Asian success, China's rise, and recent US legislation, presenting both proponent and critic perspectives. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author mentions East Asian economies primarily to

options.text: argue that their success was entirely due to free market policies
provide evidence that challenged the Washington Consensus view of development
suggest that all developing countries should copy their exact policies
contrast their failure with China's success
illustrate the dangers of excessive government intervention

questions.explanation: The East Asian economies 'deployed aggressive industrial policies' and their success 'posed a persistent challenge to this orthodoxy,' contradicting the Washington Consensus. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: Proponents of the new industrial policy, as described in the passage, argue that

options.text: government planners are always more efficient than free markets
market failures in climate change and national security justify strategic intervention
the Washington Consensus should be completely restored
China's industrial policy model should be adopted without modification
subsidies never lead to corruption or rent-seeking behavior

questions.explanation: Proponents argue that 'market failures in areas such as climate change and national security justify strategic government intervention.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: It can be inferred from the passage that the author views the question of industrial policy as

options.text: definitively settled in favor of government intervention
a complex issue with legitimate arguments on both sides
irrelevant to modern economic policymaking
primarily a concern for developing countries only
a purely academic debate with no practical implications

questions.explanation: The passage presents both critics' and proponents' arguments without endorsing either side, and frames the question as how to design effective institutions rather than whether intervention is justified. This balanced treatment matches B.

passage: Epigenetics, the study of heritable changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the underlying DNA sequence, has emerged as one of the most transformative fields in modern biology. While the genome provides the blueprint for an organism, epigenetic mechanisms determine which genes are activated or silenced in particular cells and under specific environmental conditions. The most extensively studied epigenetic mechanism is DNA methylation, in which methyl groups are attached to cytosine bases in the DNA, typically suppressing the expression of the associated gene. Histone modification, which alters the proteins around which DNA is wound, represents another key mechanism that can either promote or restrict gene access. What makes epigenetics particularly significant is the growing evidence that environmental factors such as diet, stress, toxin exposure, and even social experience can induce epigenetic changes that persist across cell divisions and, in some cases, may be transmitted to subsequent generations. Studies in rodent models have demonstrated that nutritional deficiencies in pregnant mothers can produce epigenetic alterations in their offspring that increase susceptibility to metabolic disease, effects that in some experiments have persisted into the third generation. The possibility of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance has generated considerable excitement but also controversy. Skeptics note that the evidence for transmission beyond the first generation in mammals remains limited and that many reported effects could be explained by direct exposure during pregnancy rather than true epigenetic inheritance. If confirmed, however, transgenerational epigenetic inheritance would have profound implications for our understanding of evolution, suggesting that organisms can transmit acquired adaptations to their descendants through mechanisms distinct from genetic mutation and natural selection.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: providing a detailed technical guide to DNA methylation techniques
describing the field of epigenetics and discussing the evidence and controversy surrounding transgenerational inheritance
arguing that Darwinian evolution is fundamentally incorrect
comparing epigenetic mechanisms across different animal species
recommending dietary changes to prevent epigenetic damage

questions.explanation: The passage introduces epigenetics, explains key mechanisms, discusses evidence for environmental influences and transgenerational inheritance, and notes the controversy. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, skeptics of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance argue that

options.text: epigenetic changes never occur in response to environmental factors
many reported multigenerational effects could be explained by direct exposure during pregnancy
DNA methylation has no effect on gene expression
rodent studies are always irrelevant to understanding human biology
histone modification is the only valid epigenetic mechanism

questions.explanation: Skeptics note that 'many reported effects could be explained by direct exposure during pregnancy rather than true epigenetic inheritance.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The passage suggests that confirmed transgenerational epigenetic inheritance would be significant for evolutionary theory because it would

options.text: prove that natural selection plays no role in evolution
suggest that organisms can transmit acquired adaptations through mechanisms beyond genetic mutation
eliminate the need for DNA in heredity
show that all environmental changes are permanently heritable
demonstrate that evolution occurs only through epigenetic mechanisms

questions.explanation: The passage states it would suggest 'organisms can transmit acquired adaptations to their descendants through mechanisms distinct from genetic mutation and natural selection.' This matches B, and does not eliminate natural selection's role entirely (A).

passage: The application of game theory to problems of international cooperation has yielded important insights into why nations often fail to achieve mutually beneficial outcomes. The classic framework is the Prisoner's Dilemma, in which two rational actors, each pursuing their individual self-interest, arrive at an outcome that is worse for both than would have been achievable through cooperation. In the context of international relations, this dynamic manifests in areas such as arms races, trade protectionism, and environmental degradation. The challenge of climate change provides a particularly instructive example. Every nation would benefit from a reduction in global greenhouse gas emissions, but each nation also has an incentive to free-ride on the efforts of others while continuing to develop its own economy. This structure suggests that voluntary cooperation will be insufficient to address the problem. However, the analogy between international politics and the simple Prisoner's Dilemma is imperfect. Unlike the stylized game, international interactions are repeated rather than one-shot, involve many players rather than two, and take place within institutional frameworks that can alter the payoff structure. Political scientist Robert Axelrod demonstrated through computer tournaments that in iterated Prisoner's Dilemma games, cooperative strategies such as tit-for-tat can emerge and sustain themselves over time, provided that players have a sufficiently long time horizon and can observe each other's behavior. International institutions such as the World Trade Organization and climate agreements like the Paris Accord can be understood as mechanisms designed to facilitate this kind of conditional cooperation by increasing transparency, reducing transaction costs, and creating reputational consequences for defection. Whether these institutional mechanisms are adequate to the scale of current global challenges remains a matter of vigorous debate.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: explain how game theory illuminates problems of international cooperation while noting the limitations of simple models
prove that nations will never be able to cooperate on climate change
provide a mathematical proof of the Prisoner's Dilemma
argue that the World Trade Organization should be abolished
describe Robert Axelrod's personal career in political science

questions.explanation: The passage uses game theory to explain cooperation failures, acknowledges the Prisoner's Dilemma analogy's limitations, discusses how iterated games and institutions can support cooperation, and concludes with an open question. This matches A.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, the Prisoner's Dilemma analogy is imperfect for international relations because

options.text: nations always cooperate with each other in practice
international interactions are repeated, involve many players, and occur within institutional frameworks
the mathematical assumptions of game theory are fundamentally flawed
climate change does not involve any strategic decision-making
nations cannot observe each other's behavior in any meaningful way

questions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that 'international interactions are repeated rather than one-shot, involve many players rather than two, and take place within institutional frameworks.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The passage suggests that international institutions promote cooperation primarily by

options.text: forcing nations to comply with rules through military enforcement
increasing transparency, reducing transaction costs, and creating reputational consequences
eliminating the incentive for any nation to pursue its self-interest
providing direct financial aid to nations that cooperate
replacing national governments with supranational authorities

questions.explanation: The passage describes institutions as 'mechanisms designed to facilitate conditional cooperation by increasing transparency, reducing transaction costs, and creating reputational consequences for defection.' This matches B.

passage: The growing field of neuroaesthetics seeks to understand the biological basis of aesthetic experience by studying how the brain responds to art, music, and beauty. Pioneering research by Semir Zeki has demonstrated that viewing artworks activates specific regions of the brain's reward circuitry, particularly the medial orbitofrontal cortex, the same region associated with the pleasure derived from food, romantic love, and other sources of reward. This finding suggests that aesthetic appreciation is not merely a cultural convention but is rooted in fundamental neural mechanisms shared across human populations. However, the reductionist implications of this research have provoked criticism from scholars in the humanities. Art historians and philosophers argue that reducing the experience of art to patterns of neural activation strips it of precisely those dimensions that make it meaningful: its historical context, its symbolic content, and its capacity to provoke complex intellectual and emotional responses that unfold over time rather than registering as immediate brain states. The philosopher Alva Noë has been particularly vocal in challenging what he regards as neuroaesthetics' impoverished conception of experience, arguing that perception is not something that happens in the brain but an activity that involves the whole embodied person engaging with their environment. A middle ground has been proposed by researchers who advocate for a 'neurophenomenological' approach that combines brain imaging data with detailed first-person accounts of aesthetic experience, seeking correlations without asserting that neural data exhaustively explains the richness of aesthetic engagement. This integrative strategy remains in its early stages but represents a promising attempt to bridge the gap between scientific and humanistic approaches to understanding art.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: proving that art appreciation is entirely determined by brain chemistry
describing neuroaesthetics research and the debate between scientific and humanistic perspectives on aesthetic experience
comparing the neural responses to different art forms such as painting and music
providing a biography of Semir Zeki and his contributions to neuroscience
arguing that the humanities should adopt neuroscientific methods

questions.explanation: The passage introduces neuroaesthetics, presents humanistic criticisms, and describes an integrative middle ground. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: Alva Noë's criticism of neuroaesthetics, as described in the passage, is based on the argument that

options.text: brain imaging technology is too imprecise for meaningful research
perception involves the whole embodied person, not just brain processes
art has no connection to neural activity whatsoever
aesthetic experience is identical across all cultures and individuals
neuroscientists should focus on medical applications rather than art

questions.explanation: The passage states Noë argues 'perception is not something that happens in the brain but an activity that involves the whole embodied person engaging with their environment.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The 'neurophenomenological' approach described in the passage attempts to

options.text: replace both neuroscience and art history with a new discipline
combine brain imaging data with first-person accounts of aesthetic experience
prove that humanistic interpretations of art are scientifically invalid
develop new brain scanning technologies specifically designed for art research
demonstrate that aesthetic experience has no biological basis

questions.explanation: The passage describes this approach as combining 'brain imaging data with detailed first-person accounts of aesthetic experience, seeking correlations without asserting that neural data exhaustively explains the richness of aesthetic engagement.' This matches B.

passage: The concept of algorithmic fairness has become a central concern in the deployment of machine learning systems for consequential decisions such as hiring, lending, and criminal sentencing. Researchers have identified numerous instances in which algorithms trained on historical data reproduce or even amplify existing societal biases. A widely cited study of a commercial healthcare algorithm found that it systematically underestimated the health needs of Black patients because it used healthcare spending as a proxy for health needs, and Black patients historically had less access to healthcare and therefore lower spending, even when their actual medical conditions were equally or more severe. The technical challenge of defining and achieving algorithmic fairness is compounded by the fact that multiple mathematically rigorous definitions of fairness exist, and these definitions are often mutually incompatible. Demographic parity requires that the algorithm's positive outcomes be distributed equally across groups, while equalized odds requires equal true positive and false positive rates across groups. A theorem by Chouldechova demonstrates that, except in trivial cases, these two criteria cannot be simultaneously satisfied when the base rates of the outcome differ between groups. This impossibility result means that any choice of fairness criterion involves a value judgment about which type of unfairness is more tolerable, a decision that is fundamentally ethical rather than technical. Some scholars have argued that the focus on mathematical fairness metrics obscures deeper structural inequalities and that algorithmic systems should not be deployed in high-stakes domains until the underlying social conditions that generate biased training data have been addressed. Others maintain that carefully designed algorithms, while imperfect, can reduce bias compared to unassisted human decision-making, which is itself subject to inconsistency and prejudice.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: providing a step-by-step guide for building fair machine learning algorithms
examining the technical and ethical challenges of achieving algorithmic fairness
arguing that algorithms should be banned from all decision-making processes
comparing the accuracy of different machine learning models
celebrating the progress that has been made in eliminating algorithmic bias

questions.explanation: The passage discusses bias in algorithms, the incompatibility of fairness definitions, the ethical nature of fairness choices, and contrasting views on deployment. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The healthcare algorithm example is used in the passage primarily to illustrate

options.text: the superiority of algorithmic decision-making over human judgment in healthcare
how algorithms trained on historically biased data can perpetuate existing inequities
the need for more government regulation of the healthcare industry
that Black patients receive the same quality of healthcare as other populations
the impossibility of using algorithms in any medical context

questions.explanation: The example shows that using healthcare spending as a proxy reproduced historical disparities in access. This illustrates how biased data leads to biased outcomes, matching B.

questions.questionStem: Chouldechova's theorem, as described in the passage, is significant because it demonstrates that

options.text: all algorithms are equally biased regardless of design choices
certain commonly used fairness criteria cannot be simultaneously satisfied when base rates differ between groups
demographic parity is always more important than equalized odds
machine learning should only be used for trivial applications
base rates of outcomes are always identical across demographic groups

questions.explanation: The passage states the theorem shows that 'except in trivial cases, these two criteria cannot be simultaneously satisfied when the base rates of the outcome differ between groups.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author's overall assessment of algorithmic deployment in high-stakes domains can best be described as

options.text: unequivocally supportive, arguing that algorithms always outperform humans
balanced, presenting arguments both for and against deployment while acknowledging fundamental challenges
wholly opposed, advocating a complete ban on algorithmic decision-making
indifferent, treating the issue as a purely academic exercise
focused exclusively on the mathematical dimensions of the problem

questions.explanation: The passage presents both those who argue against deployment until structural inequalities are addressed and those who maintain algorithms can reduce bias compared to human decisions. This balanced presentation matches B.

passage: Literary scholars have long debated the extent to which an author's biography should inform the interpretation of their works. The New Critics, who dominated Anglo-American literary studies in the mid-twentieth century, argued forcefully that a poem or novel should be understood as a self-contained verbal artifact, and that appeals to the author's intentions or personal circumstances constituted what W.K. Wimsatt and Monroe Beardsley termed the 'intentional fallacy.' On this view, the meaning of a text resides entirely in its language, structure, and internal logic, and biographical information is not merely irrelevant but actively misleading, tempting readers to confuse what an author meant to say with what the text actually says. Roland Barthes advanced this argument to its most radical conclusion in his influential 1967 essay, declaring metaphorically that the reader's interpretive freedom requires the conceptual elimination of the author as a controlling authority over textual meaning. However, the strict separation of text from author has been challenged from multiple directions. Feminist and postcolonial critics have argued that attending to the social position of the author, their gender, race, class, and historical location, is essential for understanding how texts both reflect and challenge structures of power. Ignoring the author's identity, they contend, risks treating literature as a disembodied intellectual exercise and obscuring the material conditions under which texts are produced and received. More recently, some scholars have advocated a pragmatic middle position, acknowledging that while biographical information should not dictate interpretation, it can serve as one among many useful contexts for enriching our understanding of literary works.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: arguing that the New Critics' approach to literature is definitively correct
tracing a debate in literary studies about the relevance of authorial biography to textual interpretation
providing a biography of W.K. Wimsatt and Monroe Beardsley
comparing the literary output of feminist and postcolonial writers
explaining how to write effective literary criticism

questions.explanation: The passage traces the debate from New Criticism through Barthes to feminist/postcolonial challenges and a pragmatic middle position. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, the 'intentional fallacy' refers to

options.text: the error of assuming that a text has no meaning at all
the mistake of using an author's intentions or personal circumstances to interpret their work
the deliberate distortion of a text's meaning by academic critics
the tendency of authors to misrepresent their own creative processes
the assumption that all literary works are autobiographical

questions.explanation: The passage defines the intentional fallacy as what occurs when 'appeals to the author's intentions or personal circumstances' are used to interpret texts. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: Feminist and postcolonial critics, as described in the passage, challenge the New Critical approach because they believe

options.text: all literary texts should be interpreted as political documents
understanding an author's social position is essential for analyzing how texts relate to structures of power
the New Critics deliberately excluded women and minority authors from the literary canon
biographical criticism produces more aesthetically pleasing interpretations
readers should identify personally with the author of every text they read

questions.explanation: The passage states these critics argue that attending to 'the social position of the author' is 'essential for understanding how texts both reflect and challenge structures of power.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The pragmatic middle position described at the end of the passage suggests that biographical information should be

options.text: the primary determinant of a text's meaning
one useful context among many, rather than the controlling factor in interpretation
completely excluded from all forms of literary criticism
used only when the author explicitly requests biographical interpretation
relevant only to works written before the twentieth century

questions.explanation: The passage states the pragmatic position acknowledges that 'biographical information should not dictate interpretation' but 'can serve as one among many useful contexts.' This matches B.

passage: The notion that cities can function as engines of innovation has been a recurring theme in economic geography since Jane Jacobs's influential works of the 1960s. Jacobs argued that the diversity of economic activities concentrated in dense urban environments generates a fertile ground for the cross-pollination of ideas, a process she termed 'knowledge spillovers.' More recent research has largely corroborated her intuition. Studies of patent data show that inventors are significantly more likely to cite the work of other inventors located in the same metropolitan area, suggesting that geographic proximity facilitates the informal exchange of knowledge. Agglomeration economies, the productivity advantages that firms derive from clustering near other firms and workers, further reinforce this pattern. However, the relationship between urban density and innovation is not without complications. Rising housing costs in major innovation hubs such as San Francisco, Boston, and London have made these cities increasingly unaffordable for young entrepreneurs and early-career researchers, potentially undermining the diversity of perspectives that Jacobs considered essential. Some scholars have observed a 'superstar city' effect in which a small number of metropolitan areas capture a disproportionate share of innovative activity, while second-tier cities struggle to attract and retain talent. The rise of remote work technologies has prompted speculation that innovation may become more geographically dispersed, but empirical evidence for this shift remains limited. Face-to-face interaction, spontaneous encounters, and the tacit knowledge embedded in dense social networks appear to confer advantages that digital communication tools have not yet replicated.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: arguing that all innovation occurs exclusively in large cities
examining the relationship between urban density and innovation, including both supporting evidence and complications
providing a biography of Jane Jacobs and her influence on urban planning
comparing housing costs across major global cities
predicting the complete decentralization of innovation due to remote work

questions.explanation: The passage discusses evidence supporting cities as innovation engines, then examines complications like housing costs, superstar city effects, and the uncertain impact of remote work. This balanced examination matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, patent citation data suggests that

options.text: most patents are filed by inventors working in isolation
geographic proximity facilitates the informal exchange of knowledge among inventors
patent quality is higher in smaller cities than in large metropolitan areas
digital communication has eliminated the need for geographic proximity
inventors deliberately avoid citing nearby colleagues to maintain competitive advantage

questions.explanation: The passage states that inventors are more likely to cite work by others 'located in the same metropolitan area, suggesting that geographic proximity facilitates the informal exchange of knowledge.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author suggests that the rise of remote work technologies

options.text: has already fundamentally redistributed innovation away from major cities
has prompted speculation about geographic dispersal but lacks strong empirical support
is irrelevant to the question of innovation geography
has made face-to-face interaction obsolete for creative collaboration
will definitely eliminate the superstar city effect within a decade

questions.explanation: The passage notes that remote work 'has prompted speculation' but that 'empirical evidence for this shift remains limited' and that face-to-face advantages persist. This matches B.

passage: The rapid decline of insect populations worldwide has alarmed ecologists, who warn that the consequences could be far-reaching and largely irreversible. A comprehensive review published in the journal Biological Conservation estimated that more than forty percent of insect species are declining globally, with a third considered endangered. The rate of decline, approximately 2.5 percent per year, suggests that many insect species could face extinction within decades if current trends continue. Insects play indispensable roles in ecosystems, serving as pollinators for roughly seventy-five percent of flowering plant species and forming the base of food webs that support birds, fish, amphibians, and other organisms. The causes of insect decline are multiple and synergistic. Habitat loss due to agricultural intensification is widely considered the most significant factor, as the conversion of natural landscapes to monoculture croplands eliminates the diverse plant communities on which many insect species depend. Pesticide use, particularly neonicotinoids, has been implicated in declines of bee and butterfly populations. Climate change compounds these pressures by altering temperature and precipitation patterns in ways that may disrupt insect life cycles and migration routes. Light pollution from urban areas has also been identified as an emerging threat, disorienting nocturnal insects and interfering with their reproductive behavior. While some insect species, particularly generalist species adapted to disturbed environments, have maintained or even increased their populations, the overall trajectory represents what some researchers have called an insect apocalypse, a term that, while contested for its alarmism, underscores the severity of the observed trends.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: propose specific solutions for reversing insect population declines
describe the scope, causes, and ecological significance of declining insect populations
argue that the term 'insect apocalypse' is an appropriate characterization of current trends
compare insect population trends across different continents
explain the biology of insect reproduction and life cycles in detail

questions.explanation: The passage covers the scale of decline, insects' ecological roles, the multiple causes (habitat loss, pesticides, climate change, light pollution), and briefly notes the contested terminology. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, neonicotinoids have been specifically linked to declines in

options.text: all insect species equally
bee and butterfly populations
nocturnal insect species that are affected by light pollution
generalist species adapted to disturbed environments
fish and amphibian populations that consume insects

questions.explanation: The passage states that neonicotinoid use 'has been implicated in declines of bee and butterfly populations.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author's attitude toward the term 'insect apocalypse' can best be described as

options.text: fully endorsing it as scientifically precise
acknowledging its contested nature while recognizing the severity of the trends it describes
dismissing it as irresponsible exaggeration
using it to argue for immediate government action
attributing it to media sensationalism rather than scientific research

questions.explanation: The passage notes the term is 'contested for its alarmism' but 'underscores the severity of the observed trends,' indicating a nuanced acknowledgment. This matches B.

passage: The emergence of streaming platforms has fundamentally altered the economics and culture of the music industry. Before the digital era, musicians earned revenue primarily through the sale of physical media such as vinyl records, cassette tapes, and compact discs, as well as through live performances and licensing agreements. The transition to digital distribution initially took the form of paid downloads through services like iTunes, which preserved the per-unit revenue model familiar to the industry. Streaming services, however, introduced a fundamentally different paradigm. Rather than purchasing individual songs or albums, consumers pay a monthly subscription fee for access to a vast catalog of music, with artists compensated through per-stream royalty payments that are often fractions of a cent. This model has been enormously profitable for the platforms themselves and has expanded access to music for consumers, but it has provoked significant criticism from artists who argue that per-stream payments are inadequate to sustain a career in music. Independent musicians and mid-tier artists have been particularly affected, as the royalty structure tends to concentrate revenue among the most popular artists whose songs accumulate billions of streams. In response, some musicians have sought to diversify their income through merchandise sales, direct-to-fan platforms, and an increased emphasis on live touring. Others have advocated for regulatory intervention to establish minimum per-stream royalty rates. The long-term impact of streaming on musical creativity remains debated, with some observers worrying that the emphasis on algorithmic discoverability may incentivize formulaic production over artistic experimentation.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: argue that streaming platforms should be shut down to protect musicians
describe how streaming has changed the economics of the music industry and the responses it has provoked
compare the sound quality of vinyl records and digital streams
explain the technology behind music streaming algorithms
celebrate the benefits of streaming for music consumers

questions.explanation: The passage traces the transition from physical media to streaming, discusses the economic impact on artists, and notes various responses and debates. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, independent and mid-tier musicians are particularly affected by streaming because

options.text: they lack the technological skills to upload their music to platforms
the royalty structure concentrates revenue among the most popular artists
streaming platforms refuse to host their music
they earn more from physical media sales than from any digital format
their music is excluded from algorithmic recommendations entirely

questions.explanation: The passage states 'the royalty structure tends to concentrate revenue among the most popular artists whose songs accumulate billions of streams,' disadvantaging independent and mid-tier artists. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author mentions concerns about algorithmic discoverability primarily to suggest that

options.text: algorithms have no influence on what music consumers listen to
the streaming model may incentivize formulaic production at the expense of artistic experimentation
streaming platforms deliberately suppress innovative music
all algorithmically recommended music is of low quality
musicians should avoid submitting their work to streaming platforms

questions.explanation: The passage notes that 'the emphasis on algorithmic discoverability may incentivize formulaic production over artistic experimentation.' This matches B.

passage: Coral reefs, often called the rainforests of the sea, support an extraordinary diversity of marine life despite covering less than one percent of the ocean floor. These complex ecosystems are built over centuries by tiny coral polyps that secrete calcium carbonate skeletons, creating the intricate three-dimensional structures that provide habitat for thousands of species of fish, invertebrates, and algae. Beyond their ecological significance, coral reefs provide substantial economic benefits to human communities. An estimated five hundred million people worldwide depend on reefs for food, coastal protection, and tourism revenue. However, coral reefs are under severe and accelerating threat. Rising ocean temperatures, driven by climate change, trigger a phenomenon known as coral bleaching, in which stressed corals expel the symbiotic algae that provide them with both color and the majority of their nutrition. Without these algae, the corals appear white and, if conditions do not improve within a few weeks, often die. The 2016 and 2017 bleaching events on Australia's Great Barrier Reef affected approximately two-thirds of the reef system, causing unprecedented mortality. Ocean acidification, another consequence of rising atmospheric carbon dioxide levels, compounds the problem by reducing the availability of calcium carbonate, making it more difficult for corals to build and maintain their skeletons. While conservation efforts including marine protected areas and coral restoration programs have achieved localized successes, scientists warn that without significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, the majority of the world's coral reefs could be lost by mid-century.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: describe the biology of coral polyps in technical detail
explain the ecological and economic importance of coral reefs and the threats they face
compare coral reef ecosystems to tropical rainforests
evaluate the effectiveness of marine protected areas around the world
argue that tourism is the primary threat to coral reef health

questions.explanation: The passage covers reefs' ecological and economic importance, then details threats from bleaching, acidification, and notes that conservation alone is insufficient without emission reductions. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, coral bleaching occurs when

options.text: pollution from coastal development poisons the coral tissue
stressed corals expel symbiotic algae that provide nutrition and color
ocean currents shift and reduce water temperatures below normal levels
fish populations consume the coral polyps and expose the white skeleton
ocean acidification dissolves the calcium carbonate skeleton entirely

questions.explanation: The passage states that bleaching occurs when 'stressed corals expel the symbiotic algae that provide them with both color and the majority of their nutrition.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author's tone regarding the future of coral reefs can best be described as

options.text: optimistic, suggesting that conservation efforts will fully reverse the decline
alarmed, emphasizing the severity of threats while noting that conservation alone is insufficient
indifferent, treating reef decline as an inevitable natural process
celebratory, focusing on recent restoration successes
angry, blaming specific governments for failing to protect reefs

questions.explanation: The author notes severe threats, unprecedented mortality, and warns that reefs could be lost by mid-century without emission reductions, while acknowledging localized conservation successes. This alarmed but factual tone matches B.

passage: The debate over the value of a liberal arts education in an era of rapidly evolving labor markets has intensified in recent years. Critics argue that traditional liberal arts curricula, with their emphasis on humanities, social sciences, and foundational sciences, fail to equip graduates with the specific technical skills demanded by employers in fields such as software engineering, data analysis, and healthcare technology. The growing burden of student loan debt has made this argument more urgent, as families question whether a degree in philosophy or literature provides sufficient return on investment to justify its cost. Proponents of the liberal arts counter that this critique rests on a narrow and ultimately short-sighted understanding of the purpose of higher education. They argue that the capacities cultivated by a liberal arts education, including critical thinking, effective communication, ethical reasoning, and the ability to synthesize information across disciplines, are precisely the skills most valued in a rapidly changing economy where specific technical knowledge quickly becomes obsolete. Research from the Association of American Colleges and Universities has found that employers consistently rank these broad competencies among their highest priorities when hiring, even as they simultaneously express desire for candidates with technical training. Furthermore, advocates note that many successful leaders in business and technology, including numerous Silicon Valley executives, hold liberal arts degrees. The resolution of this debate may lie not in choosing between liberal and technical education but in developing integrated curricula that combine disciplinary depth with broad intellectual preparation, an approach that a growing number of universities are beginning to adopt.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily structured as

options.text: a historical account of how liberal arts curricula developed over time
a presentation of opposing arguments about the value of liberal arts education, followed by a suggested resolution
a detailed comparison of specific university programs
an argument that liberal arts degrees should be eliminated
a statistical analysis of graduate employment outcomes

questions.explanation: The passage presents critics' arguments, proponents' counterarguments, and suggests integrated curricula as a resolution. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: Proponents of liberal arts education argue that its value lies partly in developing

options.text: specialized technical skills that are immediately applicable to specific jobs
broad competencies such as critical thinking and communication that remain relevant as technical knowledge changes
social connections with wealthy alumni networks
physical fitness and athletic abilities valued by employers
artistic talent that can be directly monetized in the creative industries

questions.explanation: Proponents argue liberal arts cultivates 'critical thinking, effective communication, ethical reasoning, and the ability to synthesize information' that are valuable in a changing economy. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The suggested resolution described at the end of the passage involves

options.text: eliminating all technical programs from universities
developing curricula that combine disciplinary depth with broad intellectual preparation
reducing tuition costs for liberal arts programs only
requiring all students to earn degrees in both liberal arts and engineering
allowing employers to design university curricula directly

questions.explanation: The passage suggests the resolution may be 'integrated curricula that combine disciplinary depth with broad intellectual preparation.' This matches B.

passage: The phenomenon of antibiotic resistance has been described by the World Health Organization as one of the greatest threats to global health in the twenty-first century. Antibiotics, which revolutionized medicine when they were first introduced in the 1940s, are becoming progressively less effective as bacteria evolve mechanisms to survive exposure to these drugs. The process of resistance development is driven by natural selection: when a population of bacteria is exposed to an antibiotic, most individuals are killed, but those with genetic mutations or acquired resistance genes survive and reproduce, passing their resistance to subsequent generations. The overuse and misuse of antibiotics in both human medicine and animal agriculture have dramatically accelerated this evolutionary process. In many countries, antibiotics are prescribed for viral infections against which they are ineffective, used prophylactically in healthy livestock to promote growth, or available without prescription, all practices that increase selective pressure favoring resistant strains. The consequences are already severe. Infections caused by multidrug-resistant organisms are associated with significantly higher mortality rates, longer hospital stays, and increased healthcare costs. The emergence of bacteria resistant to last-resort antibiotics such as colistin has raised the specter of a post-antibiotic era in which common infections and minor injuries could once again prove fatal. The pipeline of new antibiotics has slowed dramatically, as pharmaceutical companies have shifted investment toward more profitable therapeutic areas. Addressing the crisis will require coordinated international action encompassing improved antibiotic stewardship, investment in new drug development, rapid diagnostic technologies, and fundamental changes in agricultural practices.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: explain the scientific mechanism of a single antibiotic drug
describe the growing crisis of antibiotic resistance, its causes, and the challenges of addressing it
compare the healthcare systems of different countries
argue that animal agriculture should be completely eliminated
celebrate the historical achievements of antibiotic medicine

questions.explanation: The passage covers the mechanism of resistance, causes of acceleration (overuse in medicine and agriculture), consequences (higher mortality, healthcare costs), and the need for coordinated action. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, the development pipeline for new antibiotics has slowed primarily because

options.text: scientists have exhausted all possible chemical compounds for antibiotic development
pharmaceutical companies have redirected investment toward more profitable areas
government regulations prohibit the development of new antibiotics
existing antibiotics remain fully effective against all known bacteria
patients prefer natural remedies over pharmaceutical antibiotics

questions.explanation: The passage states 'pharmaceutical companies have shifted investment toward more profitable therapeutic areas.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The reference to a 'post-antibiotic era' most likely serves to

options.text: describe a historical period before the discovery of antibiotics
emphasize the potential severity of the resistance crisis by suggesting a return to conditions where common infections could be fatal
celebrate the development of alternative treatments that will replace antibiotics
argue that antibiotics were never truly effective against bacterial infections
predict the exact date when antibiotics will cease to work entirely

questions.explanation: The 'post-antibiotic era' is described as one 'in which common infections and minor injuries could once again prove fatal,' emphasizing the severity of the threat. This matches B.

passage: The concept of universal basic income, a regular cash payment provided to all citizens regardless of employment status or income level, has migrated from the margins of economic thought to the center of mainstream policy debate. Proponents argue that a basic income would provide a crucial safety net in an era of increasing economic precarity, eliminating the bureaucratic complexity and stigma associated with traditional means-tested welfare programs. They further contend that by providing a guaranteed floor of financial security, a basic income would empower individuals to pursue education, entrepreneurship, and caregiving activities that contribute to social well-being but are not adequately rewarded by the market. Several small-scale pilot programs have produced encouraging results. Finland's two-year experiment found that recipients reported improved well-being and modest increases in employment, while a program in Stockton, California demonstrated that monthly payments of five hundred dollars led to increased full-time employment among recipients. Critics, however, raise several objections. The most prominent concern is cost: providing a meaningful basic income to all citizens of a large country would require enormous fiscal resources, potentially necessitating significant tax increases or reductions in other public services. Some economists also worry about potential inflationary effects if a large influx of spending power drives up prices, particularly for housing and essential goods. Others question whether the results of small pilot programs, which are conducted under controlled conditions and with limited duration, can be reliably extrapolated to nationwide implementation.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: argue definitively in favor of implementing universal basic income
present the arguments for and against universal basic income while noting evidence from pilot programs
analyze the specific tax policies needed to fund a basic income program
compare the welfare systems of Finland, the United States, and other countries
explain why universal basic income has been rejected by all major economists

questions.explanation: The passage presents proponents' arguments, pilot program results, and critics' concerns. This balanced treatment matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, Finland's basic income pilot found that recipients experienced

options.text: dramatic increases in income and complete elimination of poverty
improved well-being and modest increases in employment
significant declines in motivation to work
no measurable changes in any outcome
increased reliance on additional government welfare programs

questions.explanation: The passage states Finland's experiment found 'improved well-being and modest increases in employment.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: Critics who question the extrapolation of pilot program results are most concerned that

options.text: the pilot programs were conducted in countries with different economic systems
results from small, controlled, time-limited programs may not apply to nationwide implementation
the participants in pilot programs were not representative of any population
the data from pilot programs was deliberately manipulated
pilot programs are too expensive relative to their research value

questions.explanation: The passage notes critics question 'whether the results of small pilot programs, which are conducted under controlled conditions and with limited duration, can be reliably extrapolated to nationwide implementation.' This matches B.

passage: The history of the periodic table illustrates how scientific classification systems evolve as new knowledge emerges. When Dmitri Mendeleev published his first periodic table in 1869, he arranged the sixty-three known elements by atomic weight and grouped them according to similarities in their chemical properties. Mendeleev's genius lay not merely in recognizing patterns among known elements but in his bold prediction that gaps in his table corresponded to elements that had not yet been discovered. He described the expected properties of these missing elements with remarkable accuracy, and the subsequent discovery of gallium, scandium, and germanium, each matching his predictions, dramatically validated his framework. However, Mendeleev's arrangement by atomic weight produced several anomalies. Tellurium, for example, had a higher atomic weight than iodine but clearly belonged in the group before it based on its chemical behavior. This puzzle was resolved only in the early twentieth century, when Henry Moseley demonstrated that the fundamental organizing principle of the periodic table should be atomic number, the number of protons in the nucleus, rather than atomic weight. This revision preserved the overall structure of Mendeleev's table while eliminating its inconsistencies, a testament to the robustness of the underlying pattern he had identified. The periodic table has continued to expand as physicists have synthesized increasingly heavy elements, with oganesson, element 118, completing the seventh period in 2016. Whether the table can be extended further remains an open question, as the extreme instability of superheavy elements poses fundamental challenges to both their creation and classification.
passageTitle: The Evolution of the Periodic Table

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: argue that Mendeleev deserves more credit than Moseley for the periodic table
trace the development and refinement of the periodic table as an example of evolving scientific classification
explain the chemical properties of gallium, scandium, and germanium
predict which new elements will be discovered in the future
describe the techniques used to synthesize superheavy elements

questions.explanation: The passage traces the table's development from Mendeleev's original arrangement through Moseley's revision to modern extensions, illustrating how classification evolves with knowledge. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, Mendeleev's periodic table was particularly remarkable because he

options.text: discovered more new elements than any other scientist of his era
predicted the properties of undiscovered elements based on gaps in his arrangement
used atomic number rather than atomic weight as his organizing principle
was the first scientist to identify the concept of chemical elements
completed the synthesis of all naturally occurring elements

questions.explanation: The passage highlights that Mendeleev predicted 'gaps in his table corresponded to elements that had not yet been discovered' and described their properties accurately. This matches B. Atomic number was Moseley's contribution (C).

questions.questionStem: The tellurium-iodine anomaly is mentioned in the passage to illustrate

options.text: a flaw in Mendeleev's arrangement that was later resolved by using atomic number
the impossibility of creating a fully accurate classification of elements
Mendeleev's failure to recognize basic chemical properties
the superiority of chemical behavior over any numerical ordering system
a deliberate error that Mendeleev introduced to test other scientists

questions.explanation: The anomaly showed that atomic weight produced inconsistencies, which were 'resolved only in the early twentieth century' by Moseley's use of atomic number. This matches A.

passage: The privatization of space exploration, once considered a fringe proposition, has become a defining feature of the contemporary aerospace industry. For much of the twentieth century, access to space was the exclusive domain of national governments, with the United States and the Soviet Union investing enormous public resources in programs driven by geopolitical competition and scientific prestige. The entry of private companies such as SpaceX, Blue Origin, and Rocket Lab has disrupted this paradigm by demonstrating that commercial entities can develop and operate launch vehicles at a fraction of the cost of government programs. SpaceX's development of reusable rocket technology, exemplified by the Falcon 9's ability to land and fly again, has been particularly transformative, reducing the per-kilogram cost of reaching low Earth orbit by an order of magnitude compared to the Space Shuttle era. This cost reduction has enabled a proliferation of commercial activities in space, from satellite internet constellations to space tourism flights. However, the privatization trend raises important questions about governance and equity. The Outer Space Treaty of 1967 establishes that space is the province of all humankind and cannot be claimed by any nation, but it was drafted before the era of commercial space activity and does not adequately address the activities of private companies. Issues such as the mining of asteroid resources, the management of orbital debris, and the allocation of orbital slots require updated regulatory frameworks that balance commercial innovation with the principle that space should benefit humanity broadly. The risk that space becomes primarily an arena for wealthy individuals and corporations rather than a shared human endeavor remains a significant concern.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: providing a technical explanation of reusable rocket technology
describing the shift toward private space exploration and the governance questions it raises
comparing the space programs of the United States and the Soviet Union
arguing that all space activity should return to government control
predicting which company will be the first to reach Mars

questions.explanation: The passage covers the transition from government to private space activity, discusses cost reductions and commercial proliferation, and then raises governance and equity concerns. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The Outer Space Treaty is mentioned in the passage primarily to illustrate

options.text: the superiority of international law over national legislation
the inadequacy of existing legal frameworks for addressing commercial space activities
the success of Cold War-era diplomacy in preventing space conflicts
the impossibility of regulating private companies in any domain
the need for a complete ban on commercial space activity

questions.explanation: The passage notes the treaty 'was drafted before the era of commercial space activity and does not adequately address the activities of private companies,' illustrating the regulatory gap. This matches B.

questions.questionStem: The author's overall tone regarding the privatization of space exploration can best be described as

options.text: uncritically celebratory of commercial achievements
balanced, acknowledging benefits while raising concerns about governance and equity
deeply pessimistic about the future of space exploration
hostile toward all private space companies
indifferent to the implications of commercial space activity

questions.explanation: The author acknowledges cost reductions and commercial innovation while also raising concerns about governance, equity, and the risk that space benefits only the wealthy. This balanced approach matches B.

passage: The epistemological status of economic models has been a persistent source of contention within the philosophy of science. Unlike the controlled experiments available to physicists and chemists, economists must typically rely on observational data generated by complex social systems in which relevant variables cannot be isolated or manipulated at will. This limitation has given rise to a distinctive methodology in which highly simplified mathematical models serve as the primary tools of theoretical reasoning. Milton Friedman's influential essay of 1953 argued that the realism of a model's assumptions is irrelevant; what matters is its predictive accuracy. On this instrumentalist view, a model that assumes perfectly rational agents in frictionless markets is justified if it generates predictions that approximate observed outcomes, regardless of whether its premises accurately describe real human behavior or institutional arrangements. This position has been vigorously contested. Critics from the philosophy of science argue that predictive success without mechanistic understanding offers no genuine explanation and provides an unreliable basis for policy interventions, since a model that predicts well under current conditions may fail catastrophically when conditions change. Tony Lawson's critical realist approach goes further, contending that the persistent failure of mainstream economic models to predict major events such as financial crises reflects not merely bad luck or insufficient data but a fundamental mismatch between the mathematical methods employed and the open, structured, and inherently social character of economic reality. Lawson advocates for a methodological reorientation toward qualitative and institutional analysis that takes seriously the causal mechanisms operating in specific historical and social contexts, rather than seeking universal laws expressed in mathematical form.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: defending Friedman's instrumentalist view of economic methodology
examining philosophical disagreements about the status and validity of economic models
providing a historical account of the development of mathematical economics
arguing that economics should adopt the experimental methods of physics
comparing the predictive accuracy of different economic models

questions.explanation: The passage presents Friedman's instrumentalism, critics' objections about explanation and reliability, and Lawson's critical realist alternative. This examination of philosophical disagreements matches B.

questions.questionStem: According to Friedman's view, as described in the passage, the primary criterion for evaluating an economic model is

options.text: the mathematical elegance of its equations
its predictive accuracy, regardless of whether its assumptions are realistic
the degree to which it incorporates qualitative institutional analysis
its ability to explain the causal mechanisms underlying economic phenomena
the consensus of economists about its theoretical foundations

questions.explanation: The passage states Friedman argued that 'the realism of a model's assumptions is irrelevant; what matters is its predictive accuracy.' This matches B.

questions.questionStem: Lawson's critical realist approach differs from Friedman's position primarily in that Lawson

options.text: advocates for more complex mathematical models with additional variables
argues that the mathematical methods of mainstream economics are fundamentally mismatched with the nature of economic reality
believes that economic predictions are impossible under any methodology
supports the use of controlled experiments as the only valid method in economics
agrees with Friedman but adds that models should also be aesthetically pleasing

questions.explanation: Lawson contends there is 'a fundamental mismatch between the mathematical methods employed and the open, structured, and inherently social character of economic reality,' and advocates qualitative analysis. This fundamentally contrasts with Friedman's instrumentalism, matching B.

questions.questionStem: Critics of Friedman's instrumentalism, as described in the passage, would most likely agree that

options.text: predictive accuracy is the only goal worth pursuing in any science
a model that predicts well under current conditions may fail when conditions change if it lacks mechanistic understanding
all economic models are equally valid as long as they are mathematically consistent
economic reality can be fully captured by a sufficiently complex mathematical model
financial crises are entirely unpredictable events that no methodology could anticipate

questions.explanation: Critics argue that 'predictive success without mechanistic understanding offers no genuine explanation and provides an unreliable basis for policy interventions, since a model that predicts well under current conditions may fail catastrophically when conditions change.' This matches B.

passage: The phenomenon of 'greenwashing'—the practice of making misleading claims about the environmental benefits of a product, service, or company—has become a significant concern as consumer demand for sustainable products has grown. A 2021 European Commission study found that over half of environmental claims made by companies in the EU were vague, misleading, or unfounded. Common greenwashing tactics include using vague terms such as 'eco-friendly' or 'natural' without substantiation, highlighting a single green attribute while ignoring more significant environmental impacts, and creating company-sponsored sustainability certifications that lack independent verification. The proliferation of greenwashing poses several risks. For consumers, it undermines the ability to make genuinely informed purchasing decisions. For companies that have made legitimate investments in sustainability, it creates unfair competitive pressure from rivals that gain reputational benefits without incurring the associated costs. For the broader environmental movement, it erodes public trust in sustainability claims, potentially leading to cynicism that discourages environmentally responsible behavior. Regulatory responses have varied. The U.S. Federal Trade Commission has issued 'Green Guides' that provide guidance on the use of environmental marketing claims, though these are not legally binding. The EU has proposed more stringent legislation that would require companies to substantiate environmental claims using standardized methodologies and would ban generic claims such as 'green' or 'climate-neutral' unless supported by verified evidence. Some industry groups argue that overly prescriptive regulation could stifle genuine environmental innovation by imposing excessive compliance costs, while consumer advocates contend that only rigorous standards can restore meaningful choice in the marketplace.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: describe the phenomenon of greenwashing, its consequences, and regulatory responses
argue that all environmental marketing claims are fraudulent
compare the environmental policies of the United States and the European Union
explain how consumers can identify sustainable products
evaluate the effectiveness of specific corporate sustainability programs

questions.explanation: The passage defines greenwashing, discusses tactics and risks, and presents regulatory responses.

questions.questionStem: Greenwashing is harmful to legitimately sustainable companies because it

options.text: forces them to lower their prices below cost
allows competitors to gain reputational advantages without making genuine environmental investments
makes their products less environmentally effective
prevents them from obtaining government subsidies
causes them to lose their sustainability certifications

questions.explanation: It 'creates unfair competitive pressure from rivals that gain reputational benefits without incurring the associated costs.'

questions.questionStem: The debate over regulating environmental marketing claims involves a tension between

options.text: consumer protection through rigorous standards and concerns about stifling innovation
domestic and international regulatory authority
the interests of large and small companies
environmental protection and economic growth generally
scientific research and public opinion

questions.explanation: Industry groups worry about stifling innovation while consumer advocates say 'only rigorous standards can restore meaningful choice.'

passage: The theory of plate tectonics, now the unifying paradigm of geology, was initially met with widespread skepticism when Alfred Wegener first proposed the idea of continental drift in 1912. Wegener assembled a compelling array of evidence—matching coastlines, identical fossils on widely separated continents, and geological similarities across ocean basins—to argue that the continents had once formed a single supercontinent, which he called Pangaea, and had subsequently drifted apart. However, Wegener's theory was rejected by most geologists of his era, primarily because he could not identify a plausible mechanism capable of moving entire continents across the Earth's surface. The forces he proposed, including tidal forces and centrifugal effects from Earth's rotation, were easily shown to be insufficient. It was not until the 1960s, more than three decades after Wegener's death, that the discovery of seafloor spreading provided the missing mechanism. Harry Hess and Robert Dietz demonstrated that new oceanic crust is continuously created at mid-ocean ridges and destroyed at subduction zones, providing the convective engine that moves tectonic plates. The rapid acceptance of plate tectonics once a credible mechanism was established illustrates an important epistemological principle in science: empirical evidence alone is often insufficient to establish a theory; a plausible causal mechanism is typically required as well. This principle has implications for contemporary scientific debates. In fields where strong correlational evidence exists but the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood—as in certain areas of nutrition science or epidemiology—the absence of a clear mechanism often leads to persistent scientific controversy despite substantial observational data.
passageTitle: Plate Tectonics and the Role of Mechanism in Science

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: argue that Alfred Wegener deserved more recognition during his lifetime
use the history of plate tectonics to illustrate the importance of causal mechanisms in scientific acceptance
explain the geological processes involved in seafloor spreading
compare different theories of continental movement
criticize the geology community for being resistant to new ideas

questions.explanation: The passage uses plate tectonics history to illustrate that 'a plausible causal mechanism is typically required' alongside evidence.

questions.questionStem: Wegener's theory was rejected primarily because

options.text: his evidence for continental drift was weak and unconvincing
he could not explain what force was capable of moving continents
other scientists had already proposed a better theory
the concept of Pangaea was considered politically controversial
geological technology was not advanced enough to test his claims

questions.explanation: His theory was rejected 'primarily because he could not identify a plausible mechanism capable of moving entire continents.'

questions.questionStem: The history of plate tectonics is relevant to contemporary scientific debates because

options.text: modern geology still relies on Wegener's original theory without modification
fields with strong correlational evidence but poorly understood mechanisms face similar challenges of acceptance
all scientific theories require exactly the same type of evidence
contemporary geologists continue to dispute plate tectonics
nutrition science has directly applied plate tectonic principles

questions.explanation: The passage draws a parallel to fields 'where strong correlational evidence exists but the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood.'

questions.questionStem: The author describes Wegener's evidence as 'compelling' most likely to emphasize that

options.text: Wegener was a better scientist than his contemporaries recognized
strong evidence without a convincing mechanism was still not sufficient for scientific acceptance
the evidence for continental drift has since been disproven
geologists of the early twentieth century were incapable of evaluating evidence
only geological evidence from fossils is relevant to scientific theories

questions.explanation: Calling evidence 'compelling' while noting rejection underscores that 'empirical evidence alone is often insufficient.'

passage: The Amazon rainforest, often referred to as 'the lungs of the Earth,' encompasses approximately 5.5 million square kilometers across nine South American countries, with the majority located in Brazil. It is the world's largest tropical rainforest and harbors an estimated ten percent of all species on Earth, including more than 80,000 plant species, 2.5 million insect species, and 2,000 species of birds and mammals. The forest plays a vital role in the global carbon cycle, storing an estimated 150 to 200 billion tons of carbon in its biomass and soils. The Amazon also generates approximately half of its own rainfall through a process called 'flying rivers,' in which moisture released by trees through transpiration is recycled as precipitation deeper inland. Deforestation threatens all of these ecological functions. Between 2001 and 2020, the Amazon lost approximately 400,000 square kilometers of forest cover, an area larger than Germany. The primary drivers of deforestation include cattle ranching, which accounts for roughly 80 percent of cleared land, followed by soybean cultivation and illegal logging.
