institutional quality has no relationship to economic outcomes
only wealthy countries can develop strong institutions

questions.explanation: Countries with 'strong property rights, transparent governance, progressive tax systems, and investments in universal education' have distributed growth 'more equitably.'

questions.questionStem: The final sentence of the passage suggests that some economists now believe inequality

options.text: is always necessary for economic growth
can itself become a barrier to sustained economic growth
has no effect on economic development
only matters in developed countries
is entirely caused by international trade

questions.explanation: 'Inequality itself can become an obstacle to sustained growth' by limiting human capital, reducing demand, and generating instability.

passage: The concept of the 'metaverse'—a persistent, shared virtual world in which users can interact through digital avatars—has generated enormous excitement and investment in the technology sector. Meta, formerly Facebook, committed over $36 billion to metaverse development between 2020 and 2023, while other major technology companies including Microsoft, Apple, and Google have invested billions more in related hardware and software. Proponents envision the metaverse as the successor to the mobile internet, a three-dimensional digital space where people will work, socialize, shop, and be entertained using virtual and augmented reality technologies. However, the gap between this vision and current reality remains substantial. Existing virtual reality headsets remain bulky, expensive, and prone to causing motion sickness in extended use. The computing power required to render realistic, persistent virtual environments at the scale envisioned by metaverse advocates exceeds what current technology can deliver affordably. User adoption has been disappointing; Meta's flagship metaverse platform, Horizon Worlds, reportedly attracted fewer than 200,000 monthly active users in its first year—a fraction of the billions who use existing social media platforms. Some technology analysts draw parallels with earlier periods of technology hype, noting that the metaverse concept may, like virtual reality before it, experience a prolonged 'trough of disillusionment' before eventually finding practical applications. Enterprise applications—virtual meeting spaces, training simulations, and collaborative design environments—have shown more promise than consumer-facing social platforms. Some commentators argue that the metaverse is not a specific product to be built but rather a gradual evolution of existing digital experiences that will unfold over decades rather than years.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: assess the metaverse concept by comparing its ambitious vision with current technological and adoption realities
argue that the metaverse will definitely replace the current internet
compare the business strategies of Meta and Microsoft
explain the technical workings of virtual reality headsets
evaluate which metaverse platform will be most commercially successful

questions.explanation: The passage covers the vision, investment, and then contrasts it with current limitations and disappointing adoption.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, user adoption of current metaverse platforms has been

options.text: overwhelming, exceeding all expectations
disappointing, with far fewer users than existing social media platforms
impossible to measure
exactly as predicted by technology analysts
strong among older users but weak among younger users

questions.explanation: Horizon Worlds attracted 'fewer than 200,000 monthly active users—a fraction of the billions who use existing social media platforms.'

questions.questionStem: The passage suggests that the metaverse may eventually find its most practical applications in

options.text: consumer social networking and entertainment
enterprise uses such as virtual meetings, training, and collaborative design
replacing all in-person human interaction
physical product manufacturing
space exploration and deep-sea research

questions.explanation: 'Enterprise applications—virtual meeting spaces, training simulations, and collaborative design environments—have shown more promise.'

passage: The engineering achievements of ancient Rome remain among the most impressive in human history, with many Roman structures still standing and functional after two thousand years. Roman engineers developed a sophisticated understanding of materials science, hydraulics, and structural design that would not be surpassed in Europe until the modern era. Perhaps their most significant innovation was the widespread use of concrete, a building material made from volcanic ash, lime, and seawater that proved remarkably durable. Recent research has shown that Roman concrete actually becomes stronger over time as seawater interacts with the volcanic ash to form mineral crystals that reinforce the material—a property that modern concrete, which deteriorates when exposed to seawater, does not possess. Roman aqueducts represent another remarkable engineering achievement. These structures transported fresh water over distances of up to 100 kilometers using gravity alone, with carefully calculated gradients that maintained water flow across valleys, mountains, and plains. The aqueduct system serving the city of Rome delivered an estimated one million cubic meters of water daily, providing residents with a water supply per capita comparable to that of many modern cities. Roman road construction was equally impressive in its sophistication and scale. The Roman road network eventually extended over 80,000 kilometers, connecting every corner of the empire. Roads were constructed in multiple layers—a foundation of large stones, a layer of gravel, and a surface of fitted paving stones—designed to facilitate drainage and withstand heavy traffic. Many of these roads formed the foundations for modern European highways and remain traceable on contemporary maps.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: describing the major engineering achievements of ancient Rome and their lasting significance
arguing that modern engineering is inferior to Roman engineering
comparing Roman and Greek architectural styles
explaining the political organization of the Roman Empire
analyzing the economic costs of Roman construction projects

questions.explanation: The passage covers concrete, aqueducts, and roads as major engineering achievements.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, Roman concrete is remarkable because it

options.text: was identical in composition to modern concrete
actually becomes stronger over time when exposed to seawater
could only be used for building small structures
was made entirely from materials found in a single location
deteriorates faster than any modern building material

questions.explanation: Roman concrete 'actually becomes stronger over time as seawater interacts with the volcanic ash to form mineral crystals.'

questions.questionStem: The passage mentions that Roman aqueducts transported water using

options.text: powerful mechanical pumps
gravity alone, with carefully calculated gradients
heat from volcanic sources
pressure created by elevated water tanks
the natural flow of rivers diverted into channels

questions.explanation: Aqueducts 'transported fresh water... using gravity alone, with carefully calculated gradients.'

passage: Nuclear fusion—the process that powers the sun and stars by fusing light atomic nuclei to form heavier ones, releasing enormous amounts of energy—has been pursued as a potential energy source since the 1950s. Unlike nuclear fission, which splits heavy atoms and produces long-lived radioactive waste, fusion would generate energy from abundant hydrogen isotopes with minimal radioactive byproducts and no risk of the runaway chain reactions that caused disasters at Chernobyl and Fukushima. However, achieving controlled fusion on Earth requires creating and confining a plasma at temperatures exceeding 100 million degrees Celsius—conditions that present extraordinary engineering challenges. The two leading approaches to fusion containment are magnetic confinement, exemplified by tokamak reactors that use powerful magnetic fields to contain the plasma in a doughnut-shaped chamber, and inertial confinement, which uses powerful lasers to compress fuel pellets to extreme densities. In December 2022, the National Ignition Facility at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory achieved a significant milestone by demonstrating fusion ignition—producing more energy from the fusion reaction than was delivered to the fuel by the lasers. While this achievement was celebrated as a scientific breakthrough, the total energy consumed by the facility's laser system far exceeded the energy produced, highlighting the gap between scientific demonstration and practical energy generation. ITER, the international tokamak project under construction in France, aims to demonstrate sustained fusion power production by the mid-2030s. Meanwhile, a growing number of private companies, backed by billions in venture capital, are pursuing alternative designs with more aggressive timelines. Critics note that the phrase 'fusion is thirty years away—and always has been' captures a persistent pattern of optimistic predictions followed by delays, though proponents argue that recent advances in superconducting magnets and plasma physics have fundamentally changed the trajectory of the field.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: describe the status of nuclear fusion research, including recent breakthroughs and persistent challenges
argue that fusion energy is impossible and research should be abandoned
compare fusion energy with renewable energy sources such as solar and wind
explain the physics of nuclear reactions at a subatomic level
evaluate which private fusion company is most likely to succeed

questions.explanation: The passage covers fusion's promise, two main approaches, the NIF breakthrough, ITER, and the gap between promise and reality.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, the NIF's 2022 achievement was significant but limited because

options.text: it used fission rather than fusion
although the reaction produced more energy than was delivered to the fuel, the total energy consumed by the facility far exceeded the output
the experiment could not be repeated
the energy produced was too dangerous to be useful
the fusion reaction lasted less than one second

questions.explanation: 'The total energy consumed by the facility's laser system far exceeded the energy produced.'

questions.questionStem: The passage mentions the phrase 'fusion is thirty years away—and always has been' to

options.text: prove that fusion will never be achieved
acknowledge a persistent pattern of optimistic predictions and delays in fusion research
argue that all fusion scientists are dishonest
suggest that fusion research should receive less funding
compare fusion timelines with those of other energy technologies

questions.explanation: The phrase 'captures a persistent pattern of optimistic predictions followed by delays.'

questions.questionStem: Fusion energy differs from fission energy in that fusion

options.text: uses heavy atoms rather than light ones
would produce minimal radioactive waste and use abundant fuel without risk of runaway reactions
has already been deployed in commercial power plants worldwide
requires lower temperatures than fission
produces no energy at all

questions.explanation: Fusion generates energy 'from abundant hydrogen isotopes with minimal radioactive byproducts and no risk of runaway chain reactions.'

passage: The concept of corporate social responsibility (CSR)—the idea that businesses have obligations to society beyond maximizing shareholder value—has evolved significantly since the term gained currency in the 1960s and 1970s. Early CSR initiatives were largely philanthropic, consisting of charitable donations and community support programs that were peripheral to core business operations. Contemporary CSR, by contrast, increasingly integrates social and environmental considerations into business strategy, supply chain management, and corporate governance. The business case for CSR has been widely debated. Advocates point to research suggesting that companies with strong CSR programs enjoy benefits including enhanced brand reputation, improved employee recruitment and retention, reduced regulatory risk, and stronger relationships with communities and governments. A meta-analysis of over 2,000 studies found a modest positive correlation between CSR performance and financial performance, though the direction of causation remains contested—it may be that profitable companies simply have more resources to invest in CSR rather than CSR driving profitability. Skeptics, following Milton Friedman's influential critique, argue that CSR diverts resources from productive economic activity and represents a form of taxation without representation, as corporate managers spend shareholders' money on social objectives that shareholders may not endorse. More nuanced critics suggest that CSR can serve as a shield against scrutiny, allowing companies to publicize modest social initiatives while continuing practices—aggressive tax avoidance, lobbying against environmental regulation, or exploitative labor conditions in supply chains—that cause far greater harm than the CSR programs mitigate.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: discuss the evolution of CSR and present arguments for and against it
argue that all companies should immediately adopt comprehensive CSR programs
compare the CSR programs of specific companies
explain how to design an effective CSR strategy
demonstrate that CSR has no effect on corporate financial performance

questions.explanation: The passage covers CSR's evolution, the business case, and both supporters' and skeptics' arguments.

questions.questionStem: The meta-analysis finding of a positive correlation between CSR and financial performance is complicated by

options.text: the fact that no studies have ever examined this relationship
uncertainty about whether CSR causes profitability or profitability enables CSR
the impossibility of measuring corporate social responsibility
government regulations that require all companies to implement CSR
the finding that CSR always reduces profitability

questions.explanation: 'The direction of causation remains contested—it may be that profitable companies simply have more resources to invest in CSR.'

questions.questionStem: More nuanced critics of CSR are concerned that it can

options.text: completely transform companies into nonprofit organizations
serve as a shield that allows companies to publicize small initiatives while continuing harmful practices
make companies too transparent about their operations
prevent companies from earning any profits
only benefit companies in the technology sector

questions.explanation: CSR can allow companies 'to publicize modest social initiatives while continuing practices... that cause far greater harm.'

passage: The relationship between sleep and memory consolidation has been one of the most productive areas of neuroscience research in recent decades. Scientists have established that sleep plays a critical role in transforming newly acquired information into stable, long-term memories—a process known as memory consolidation. During waking hours, the hippocampus, a brain structure essential for forming new memories, temporarily stores recently experienced information. During sleep, particularly during slow-wave sleep stages, the hippocampus repeatedly 'replays' these stored patterns to the neocortex, the brain's long-term storage system, gradually strengthening the neural connections that encode the memory. Research using functional neuroimaging has confirmed that the same patterns of brain activity observed during learning are reactivated during subsequent sleep. Studies have shown that participants who sleep after learning new material—whether factual information, motor skills, or emotional experiences—consistently outperform those who remain awake for an equivalent period. Notably, even brief naps of 60 to 90 minutes, which include slow-wave sleep, can provide significant memory benefits. The role of REM sleep in memory is more complex. While slow-wave sleep appears primarily important for consolidating factual and spatial memories, REM sleep seems to play a larger role in processing emotional memories and integrating new information with existing knowledge to generate creative insights. Sleep deprivation, which has become increasingly common in modern societies, therefore has implications that extend beyond immediate cognitive performance to include long-term knowledge retention and creative problem-solving. These findings have practical implications for educational settings, where scheduling, homework policies, and exam timing could be redesigned to optimize the sleep-dependent learning process.
passageTitle: The Science of Sleep and Memory

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: explain how sleep contributes to memory consolidation and discuss the implications of these findings
argue that all schools should start later in the morning
compare different theories of how memory works
describe the anatomy of the brain in detail
evaluate the effectiveness of different sleep medications

questions.explanation: The passage explains the hippocampal replay mechanism, different sleep stages' roles, and practical implications.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, during slow-wave sleep, the hippocampus

options.text: stops all activity to allow the brain to rest
replays recently stored patterns to the neocortex for long-term storage
erases all memories formed during the day
creates entirely new memories not based on waking experience
sends signals only to the visual cortex

questions.explanation: 'The hippocampus repeatedly replays these stored patterns to the neocortex, the brain's long-term storage system.'

questions.questionStem: The passage suggests that REM sleep is particularly important for

options.text: consolidating factual and spatial memories
processing emotional memories and integrating new information for creative insights
physical muscle recovery and growth
regulating body temperature during sleep
preventing sleepwalking and other sleep disorders

questions.explanation: 'REM sleep seems to play a larger role in processing emotional memories and integrating new information with existing knowledge to generate creative insights.'

passage: The history of modern art has been characterized by a recurring pattern of innovation, controversy, and eventual acceptance that mirrors broader cultural attitudes toward change and tradition. When the Impressionists first exhibited their work in Paris in 1874, critics ridiculed their loose brushwork, unconventional color palettes, and departure from academic standards of realistic representation. One hostile review of Claude Monet's painting—from which the movement took its name—dismissed it as a mere 'impression' rather than a finished work of art. Within decades, however, Impressionism was embraced as a major artistic achievement, and its once-controversial techniques became foundational to subsequent artistic developments. This cycle of rejection and acceptance repeated with each subsequent movement. Cubism was derided as incomprehensible; Abstract Expressionism was attacked as formless self-indulgence; conceptual art faced the perennial complaint that 'my child could do that.' Each movement challenged prevailing assumptions about what art should look like, how it should be made, and what purposes it should serve. Yet the recurring nature of this pattern raises an important question: if avant-garde movements are consistently criticized and then canonized, does the inevitability of this cycle diminish the validity of criticism itself? Some art theorists argue that critical resistance serves a useful function even when it ultimately fails to prevent acceptance, because it forces artists and advocates to articulate the principles and values underlying new artistic approaches. Others contend that the art world's institutional structures—galleries, museums, auction houses, and academic programs—have become so effective at absorbing and legitimizing novelty that genuine critical resistance has become impossible, replaced by a market-driven cycle in which controversy itself becomes a commodity that enhances commercial value.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: describe the recurring pattern of innovation and controversy in modern art and raise questions about the role of criticism
argue that Impressionism was the most important art movement in history
compare the techniques used by different modern art movements
explain how art auction houses determine the price of paintings
propose a new framework for evaluating artistic quality

questions.explanation: The passage traces the rejection-acceptance cycle and discusses what this means for criticism.

questions.questionStem: The Impressionists' initial reception is discussed primarily to

options.text: prove that Monet was the greatest painter in history
illustrate the recurring pattern of critical rejection followed by eventual acceptance
argue that art critics are always wrong
compare French and American attitudes toward art
explain the technical aspects of Impressionist painting techniques

questions.explanation: The Impressionists' experience introduces the pattern of 'innovation, controversy, and eventual acceptance.'

questions.questionStem: Some art theorists argue that the art world's institutions have made genuine critical resistance impossible because

options.text: museums refuse to display any new artwork
institutions have become so effective at absorbing novelty that controversy itself becomes commercially valuable
art critics are no longer trained in art history
all modern art looks identical to classical art
governments have banned criticism of contemporary art

questions.explanation: Institutions 'have become so effective at absorbing and legitimizing novelty that... controversy itself becomes a commodity.'

passage: The emergence of financial technology companies—collectively known as fintech—has posed the most significant competitive challenge to traditional banking in decades. By leveraging mobile technology, data analytics, and streamlined user interfaces, fintech firms have captured market share in areas including payments processing, consumer lending, wealth management, and insurance. In payments, companies such as PayPal, Square, and Stripe have created digital payment ecosystems that bypass traditional bank-mediated transaction systems, processing trillions of dollars annually. In lending, online platforms have used algorithmic credit assessment models that analyze thousands of data points—including non-traditional indicators such as social media activity and utility payment history—to extend credit to borrowers who might be rejected by conventional banks using traditional credit scoring. The advantages of fintech firms stem largely from their lack of legacy infrastructure. Traditional banks operate extensive branch networks, maintain aging technology systems, and comply with regulatory frameworks designed for a pre-digital era, all of which create cost structures that nimbler digital-first competitors can undercut. However, fintech firms face their own challenges. As they scale, they increasingly encounter the same regulatory requirements—capital adequacy ratios, anti-money laundering compliance, consumer protection obligations—that burden traditional banks. The 2022-2023 downturn in fintech valuations, following a period of exuberant investment, demonstrated that many fintech business models had not yet proven sustainable at scale without continuous infusions of venture capital. Some observers predict that the future of financial services will be neither purely traditional nor purely digital, but rather a hybrid model in which incumbent banks and fintech firms form partnerships that combine the regulatory expertise and customer trust of established institutions with the technological agility and user experience innovation of digital challengers.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: examine how fintech companies have challenged traditional banking and discuss the challenges fintech itself faces
argue that traditional banks should be completely replaced by fintech companies
explain the specific algorithms used in fintech credit assessment
compare the stock prices of different fintech companies
describe the history of banking regulation in the United States

questions.explanation: The passage covers fintech's competitive advantages, specific sectors, and the challenges and limitations of fintech.

questions.questionStem: Fintech firms have an advantage over traditional banks primarily because they

options.text: have more physical branch locations
lack legacy infrastructure, enabling lower cost structures and more agile technology
are exempt from all financial regulations
have been in business for longer than traditional banks
only serve wealthy customers

questions.explanation: Fintech advantage stems from 'their lack of legacy infrastructure' while banks have 'extensive branch networks, aging technology systems.'

hybrid partnerships combining traditional banks' trust and regulatory expertise with fintech's technology

a return to exclusively cash-based transactions
fintech companies acquiring all traditional banks

questions.explanation: The future will be 'a hybrid model in which incumbent banks and fintech firms form partnerships.'

options.text: fintech companies are always more profitable than traditional banks
many fintech business models had not proven sustainable at scale without continuous venture capital
all financial technology is fundamentally unprofitable
investors had lost all interest in financial services
traditional banks had completely outperformed fintech companies in every metric

questions.explanation: The downturn 'demonstrated that many fintech business models had not yet proven sustainable at scale without continuous infusions of venture capital.'

passage: Advances in genetic testing technology have created unprecedented capabilities for predicting an individual's susceptibility to diseases, but these capabilities raise profound ethical questions about privacy, discrimination, and the psychological burden of predictive medical knowledge. Direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies now offer assessments for genetic variants associated with conditions ranging from breast cancer to Alzheimer's disease, often without the involvement of healthcare professionals who could provide context and counseling. The concept of 'genetic determinism'—the assumption that a positive genetic test result means a disease will inevitably develop—represents a widespread misunderstanding that can cause unwarranted anxiety. Most genetic variants identified by consumer testing confer only modestly increased risk, and the actual development of disease depends on complex interactions between multiple genes, lifestyle factors, and environmental exposures. Yet the psychological impact of receiving unfavorable genetic information can be significant, affecting mental health, life planning decisions, and family relationships. The potential for genetic discrimination adds another layer of concern. Although the United States' Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act prohibits the use of genetic information in employment and health insurance decisions, it does not cover life insurance, disability insurance, or long-term care insurance. This gap means that individuals who learn they carry genetic risk factors may face financial consequences if they disclose this information—or ethical dilemmas if they do not. Perhaps most philosophically challenging is the question of whether individuals have a 'right not to know' their genetic predispositions. Some ethicists argue that the ability to choose ignorance about one's genetic future is a fundamental aspect of autonomy, while others contend that genetic information—particularly when it has implications for blood relatives—creates moral obligations to know and to share.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: explore the ethical challenges raised by advances in genetic testing technology
argue that all genetic testing should be banned
explain the science of DNA sequencing at a molecular level
compare different direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies
evaluate the accuracy of genetic tests for specific diseases

questions.explanation: The passage covers misunderstanding, psychological impact, discrimination concerns, and the philosophical 'right not to know.'

questions.questionStem: The passage suggests that 'genetic determinism' is problematic because

options.text: genetic tests are completely inaccurate
most genetic variants confer only modestly increased risk, and disease depends on complex interactions
all genetic testing is prohibitively expensive
genes have no influence on disease development
doctors always fully explain genetic test results to patients

questions.explanation: Most variants 'confer only modestly increased risk, and actual development depends on complex interactions between multiple genes, lifestyle factors, and environmental exposures.'

questions.questionStem: The 'right not to know' one's genetic predispositions is philosophically challenging because

options.text: no one has ever refused genetic testing
it involves a tension between individual autonomy and potential moral obligations to relatives who may share genetic risks
genetic testing is mandatory in most countries
genetic information has no relevance to family members
all ethicists agree that everyone should undergo genetic testing

questions.explanation: Some see ignorance as 'a fundamental aspect of autonomy,' while others argue genetic information 'creates moral obligations to know and to share,' especially for relatives.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, a gap in U.S. genetic nondiscrimination law is that it does not cover

options.text: employment decisions based on genetic information
health insurance decisions based on genetic testing
life insurance, disability insurance, or long-term care insurance
any form of genetic discrimination whatsoever
genetic testing performed in medical facilities

questions.explanation: GINA 'does not cover life insurance, disability insurance, or long-term care insurance.'

passage: The relationship between immigration and economic growth has been the subject of extensive research and vigorous political debate. Economists have generally found that immigration contributes positively to economic growth through several channels. Immigrants expand the labor force, filling both high-skilled positions in technology, healthcare, and research, and lower-skilled roles in agriculture, construction, and service industries that domestic workers are often reluctant to fill. In the United States, immigrants have founded approximately 45 percent of Fortune 500 companies, and foreign-born workers account for a disproportionate share of patent filings and scientific publications. Immigration can also help offset the fiscal challenges posed by aging populations in developed countries, as working-age immigrants contribute tax revenue that supports pension and healthcare systems. However, the economic effects of immigration are not uniformly positive across all segments of society. Research by economist George Borjas has suggested that immigration can depress wages for native-born workers in directly competing occupations, particularly those without college degrees. While the magnitude of this wage effect is debated—other economists argue that immigrants and native workers are more complementary than substitutional—the perception of labor market competition has fueled anti-immigration sentiment in many countries. The fiscal impact of immigration also varies depending on the time horizon considered. While newly arrived immigrants may initially consume more in public services than they contribute in taxes, longitudinal studies consistently show that immigrants and their children become net fiscal contributors over the long term, particularly when immigration policies select for education and skill levels.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: present a balanced examination of the economic effects of immigration, including both benefits and concerns
argue that all immigration should be stopped to protect native workers
compare immigration policies across different countries in detail
explain the legal process for obtaining citizenship in the United States
evaluate which types of immigrants contribute most to economic growth

questions.explanation: The passage covers positive effects, concerns about wage competition, and fiscal impact over time.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, Borjas's research suggests that immigration may

options.text: always benefit all native-born workers equally
depress wages for native workers in directly competing occupations, especially those without college degrees
have no effect whatsoever on the labor market
only affect wages in the technology sector
increase wages for all workers regardless of skill level

questions.explanation: Immigration 'can depress wages for native-born workers in directly competing occupations, particularly those without college degrees.'

questions.questionStem: The fiscal impact of immigration over time, according to the passage, follows a pattern in which

options.text: immigrants are always a net fiscal burden at every stage
immigrants may initially consume more services than they contribute but become net contributors over the long term
immigrants never use any public services
fiscal impact is identical for all immigrant groups regardless of education
governments always lose revenue from immigration regardless of time horizon

questions.explanation: 'Newly arrived immigrants may initially consume more... longitudinal studies consistently show that immigrants and their children become net fiscal contributors over the long term.'

passage: Three-dimensional printing, also known as additive manufacturing, has evolved from a prototyping tool into a technology with significant industrial applications. Unlike traditional manufacturing processes that typically involve cutting, drilling, or molding material into desired shapes—a process known as subtractive manufacturing—3D printing builds objects layer by layer from digital design files. This approach offers several advantages. It enables the creation of complex geometries that would be impossible or prohibitively expensive to produce using conventional methods. It dramatically reduces waste, since material is only deposited where needed rather than removed from a larger block. And it allows for cost-effective production of small batches and customized products without the expensive tooling and molds required for mass production. The aerospace industry has been an early adopter, using 3D printing to produce lightweight components with complex internal structures that reduce aircraft weight and fuel consumption. Medical applications have also advanced rapidly, with 3D-printed prosthetics, dental implants, and surgical guides now in routine clinical use. Researchers are exploring the printing of living tissue using 'bioinks' composed of human cells, a technology that could eventually enable the production of replacement organs. However, 3D printing faces limitations. Production speeds remain significantly slower than conventional manufacturing for large volumes, making it unsuitable for mass production of simple components. The range of printable materials, while expanding, is still more limited than what traditional manufacturing can process. Quality control challenges persist, as printed parts can contain microscopic defects that affect structural integrity. Despite these limitations, the technology continues to find new applications and is increasingly viewed as a complement to rather than a replacement for conventional manufacturing.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: describing the capabilities, applications, and limitations of 3D printing technology
arguing that 3D printing will completely replace all traditional manufacturing
explaining the computer software used to create 3D printing designs
comparing the costs of different 3D printing machines
evaluating which 3D printing company is most likely to dominate the market

questions.explanation: The passage covers advantages, applications (aerospace, medical), and limitations.

questions.questionStem: 3D printing differs from traditional subtractive manufacturing primarily because it

options.text: removes material from a solid block to create shapes
builds objects layer by layer, adding material only where needed
requires more material than traditional methods
can only produce simple shapes
uses more energy than any conventional process

questions.explanation: 3D printing 'builds objects layer by layer from digital design files' rather than cutting from larger blocks.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, 3D printing is particularly useful for

options.text: mass production of millions of identical simple products
producing complex geometries, small batches, and customized products cost-effectively
only producing prototypes that cannot be used as final products
replacing all human workers in manufacturing
producing items that require no quality control

questions.explanation: It enables 'complex geometries,' reduces waste, and 'allows for cost-effective production of small batches and customized products.'

passage: The question of whether human beings possess genuine free will—the ability to have done otherwise in any given situation—has been debated by philosophers for millennia and has gained renewed urgency in light of advances in neuroscience. The traditional libertarian position holds that human agents are genuinely free; they are not merely the product of prior causes but can initiate actions through an irreducible capacity for choice. Hard determinists, by contrast, argue that every event, including every human decision, is the inevitable consequence of prior causes governed by the laws of nature. If the state of the universe at any given moment, combined with the laws of physics, necessarily determines the state of the universe at every subsequent moment, then human choices are no different in principle from the movement of billiard balls. Perhaps the most widely held position among contemporary philosophers is compatibilism, which argues that free will and determinism are not actually in conflict. Compatibilists redefine free will not as the ability to have done otherwise in an absolute sense, but rather as the ability to act according to one's own desires and reasons without external coercion. On this view, a person acts freely when their actions flow from their own deliberative processes, even if those processes are themselves the products of prior causes. Neuroscientific findings have complicated this debate further. Benjamin Libet's famous experiments in the 1980s showed that unconscious brain activity associated with a decision begins several hundred milliseconds before the subject reports becoming aware of the intention to act, suggesting that conscious awareness may be a post-hoc narrative rather than the initiating cause of action. While the interpretation of these experiments remains contested, they have fueled philosophical concern about whether the phenomenology of deliberation—the experience of weighing options and choosing—reflects the actual causal process underlying decision-making.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: survey the major philosophical positions on free will and discuss how neuroscience has complicated the debate
prove that free will definitively does not exist
explain the laws of physics governing human brain activity
compare ancient and modern views on human responsibility
propose a new philosophical framework that resolves the free will debate

questions.explanation: The passage covers libertarianism, hard determinism, compatibilism, and Libet's neuroscience findings.

questions.questionStem: Compatibilism differs from hard determinism in that compatibilism

options.text: denies that any events are caused by prior events
redefines free will as acting according to one's own desires without coercion, even within a deterministic framework
argues that physics does not apply to human brains
was developed entirely by neuroscientists rather than philosophers
holds that human choices are completely random

questions.explanation: Compatibilists redefine free will as 'the ability to act according to one's own desires and reasons without external coercion' within determinism.

questions.questionStem: Libet's experiments are significant to the free will debate because they suggest that

options.text: humans always make perfectly rational decisions
unconscious brain activity may precede conscious awareness of decisions, challenging the notion that conscious deliberation initiates action
all brain activity is random and unpredictable
free will has been scientifically proven to exist
the brain does not play any role in decision-making

questions.explanation: 'Unconscious brain activity... begins several hundred milliseconds before the subject reports becoming aware of the intention to act.'

questions.questionStem: The passage characterizes the free will debate as having 'renewed urgency' because

options.text: philosophers recently discovered the concept for the first time
neuroscientific findings have provided new empirical evidence relevant to philosophical questions about choice
courts have stopped holding people responsible for crimes
free will has been proven by quantum physics
the debate was previously considered completely resolved

questions.explanation: Advances in neuroscience have provided empirical evidence that bears on the philosophical question.

passage: The venture capital industry, which provides equity financing to early-stage companies with high growth potential in exchange for ownership stakes, has become a defining feature of modern innovation ecosystems. Silicon Valley, home to the largest concentration of venture capital firms and technology startups in the world, has served as a model for entrepreneurial ecosystems that many cities and countries have attempted to replicate. The venture capital model is characterized by a high failure rate—industry data suggests that approximately 75 percent of venture-backed startups fail to return investors' capital—offset by the outsized returns generated by a small number of spectacularly successful investments. A single investment in a company that achieves a 100x return can compensate for dozens of failures within a fund. This model has funded many of the world's most valuable technology companies, including Google, Facebook, Amazon, and Tesla. However, the venture capital model has attracted criticism on several grounds. The geographic concentration of venture funding—with the San Francisco Bay Area, New York, and Boston collectively receiving a majority of U.S. venture investment—has been criticized for neglecting promising startups in other regions. Gender and racial disparities in funding are stark: in the United States, companies with all-female founding teams received approximately two percent of venture capital funding in 2022, and Black and Latino founders received even smaller shares. Some economists have questioned whether the venture capital model, with its emphasis on rapid growth and market dominance, creates incentives that prioritize scale over sustainability, leading to business practices—aggressive subsidization, regulatory arbitrage, and winner-take-all market strategies—that may not serve broader social interests.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: describe the venture capital model, its contributions to innovation, and criticisms it has faced
argue that venture capital should be abolished
compare venture capital returns with stock market returns in detail
explain the legal structure of venture capital partnerships
evaluate which venture capital firm has the best track record

questions.explanation: The passage covers the VC model, its success, geographic and demographic disparities, and structural criticisms.

questions.questionStem: The venture capital model succeeds despite a high failure rate because

options.text: venture capitalists never lose any money
a small number of spectacularly successful investments generate returns that compensate for many failures
all venture-backed startups eventually become profitable
government subsidies cover all losses from failed investments
venture capital firms invest in hundreds of thousands of companies simultaneously

questions.explanation: 'A single investment in a company that achieves a 100x return can compensate for dozens of failures.'

questions.questionStem: The passage identifies gender and racial disparities in venture funding to illustrate that

options.text: venture capital has always been equally distributed among all demographics
companies with all-female founding teams received only about two percent of funding, highlighting significant inequity
only male-founded companies can succeed in technology
venture capital firms are legally prohibited from funding diverse founders
these disparities have been completely eliminated in recent years

questions.explanation: 'Companies with all-female founding teams received approximately two percent' and 'Black and Latino founders received even smaller shares.'

passage: Climate change is increasingly recognized as a driver of human migration and displacement, though the relationship between environmental change and population movement is more complex than straightforward cause-and-effect narratives suggest. The World Bank has projected that by 2050, approximately 216 million people could be forced to move within their own countries due to climate-related factors including sea level rise, water scarcity, declining crop productivity, and increasing frequency of extreme weather events. Small island developing states face particularly acute threats; nations such as Tuvalu, Kiribati, and the Marshall Islands could become largely uninhabitable within decades due to rising sea levels and saltwater intrusion into freshwater supplies. However, environmental factors rarely operate in isolation as drivers of migration. Climate change typically interacts with existing social, economic, and political vulnerabilities to create conditions that compel movement. Communities with greater economic resources, stronger governance institutions, and more diversified livelihoods are generally better able to adapt to environmental changes in place, while those already marginalized by poverty, conflict, or discrimination are more likely to be displaced. This intersectional understanding of climate migration has important implications for policy. It suggests that reducing vulnerability to climate-related displacement requires not only greenhouse gas emissions reduction but also investment in adaptation infrastructure, poverty reduction, and governance reform in affected regions. The legal framework for climate migrants remains underdeveloped. The 1951 Refugee Convention does not recognize environmental factors as grounds for refugee status, leaving climate-displaced people in a legal gray zone. Some scholars have proposed a new international protocol specifically for climate migrants, while others argue that expanding existing frameworks would be more politically feasible than creating entirely new ones.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: examine the complex relationship between climate change and migration and discuss policy and legal challenges
argue that climate migration is not a real phenomenon
compare migration patterns in different geographic regions
propose specific relocation plans for island nations
analyze the financial costs of climate change adaptation

questions.explanation: The passage covers projections, complexity of drivers, policy implications, and legal gaps.

questions.questionStem: The passage suggests that environmental factors as drivers of migration are

options.text: the sole and direct cause of all human movement
rarely isolated, typically interacting with social, economic, and political vulnerabilities
irrelevant to migration patterns worldwide
only significant for island nations
easily addressed through technology alone

questions.explanation: 'Environmental factors rarely operate in isolation' but 'interact with existing social, economic, and political vulnerabilities.'

questions.questionStem: The legal framework for climate migrants is described as underdeveloped because

options.text: all countries have comprehensive climate migration laws
the 1951 Refugee Convention does not recognize environmental factors as grounds for refugee status
international law explicitly prohibits all migration
no scholars have ever studied climate migration
climate change has no effect on where people live

questions.explanation: 'The 1951 Refugee Convention does not recognize environmental factors as grounds for refugee status.'

questions.questionStem: Communities that are most vulnerable to climate-related displacement tend to be those that

options.text: have the most economic resources and strongest institutions
are already marginalized by poverty, conflict, or discrimination
live in cold climates far from the ocean
have the highest levels of education and technological development
have already adapted successfully to previous environmental changes

questions.explanation: 'Those already marginalized by poverty, conflict, or discrimination are more likely to be displaced.'

passage: Podcasting, the distribution of audio content through digital platforms for on-demand listening, has grown from a niche hobby into a significant media industry. The number of active podcasts worldwide surpassed four million by 2023, covering virtually every conceivable topic from true crime to business strategy. Major technology companies have invested heavily in the medium: Spotify spent over $1 billion acquiring podcast studios and exclusive content, while Apple, Amazon, and Google have all expanded their podcast platforms. Advertising revenue in the U.S. podcast market reached approximately $2 billion in 2023 and is projected to continue growing rapidly. Several factors have driven podcasting's growth. The proliferation of smartphones has made audio content accessible anywhere, while improved voice recording technology has lowered production barriers to the point where anyone with a smartphone can create a podcast. The intimate, conversational format of many podcasts creates a sense of personal connection between hosts and listeners that traditional broadcast media often lacks. Long-form podcast episodes, which can run for two or three hours, allow for in-depth exploration of topics that would be impractical in the time constraints of television or radio. However, the podcasting industry faces challenges. Discovery remains difficult; with millions of shows available, new podcasters struggle to attract audiences. Monetization is concentrated among the most popular shows, while the vast majority of podcasts generate little or no revenue. There are also concerns about the spread of misinformation through podcasts, which operate largely outside the editorial standards and fact-checking processes that govern traditional media. Unlike written articles that can be easily searched and verified, audio content is more difficult to monitor at scale, creating accountability gaps that some observers find troubling.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: describing the growth of podcasting as a media industry, its driving factors, and challenges it faces
arguing that podcasts are superior to all other forms of media
comparing the audio quality of different podcast platforms
explaining the technical process of recording a podcast
evaluating which podcast genre is most profitable

questions.explanation: The passage covers growth, investment, driving factors, and challenges including discovery and misinformation.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, podcasting's growth has been driven in part by

options.text: the decline of all other forms of entertainment
smartphones making audio accessible and low production barriers enabling anyone to create content
government subsidies for podcast creators
the mandatory inclusion of podcasts in school curricula
a decrease in the number of available podcasts

questions.explanation: Smartphones 'made audio content accessible anywhere' and improved technology 'lowered production barriers.'

questions.questionStem: The passage identifies misinformation as a concern in podcasting because

options.text: all podcast hosts deliberately spread false information
audio content is more difficult to search and verify at scale than written content
listeners always believe everything they hear in podcasts
podcasts are regulated more strictly than newspapers
misinformation only exists in the podcasting medium

questions.explanation: 'Audio content is more difficult to monitor at scale, creating accountability gaps.'

passage: Behavioral finance, which applies insights from cognitive psychology to understand financial markets, has offered compelling explanations for the recurrence of speculative bubbles—episodes in which asset prices rise far above fundamental values before collapsing dramatically. Classical financial theory, built on the efficient market hypothesis, struggled to account for such episodes, as it assumed that rational investors would quickly identify and correct mispricings through arbitrage. Behavioral finance challenges this assumption by identifying systematic cognitive biases that can drive prices away from fundamentals for extended periods. Herding behavior—the tendency of investors to follow the actions of others rather than conducting independent analysis—can create self-reinforcing price increases as rising prices attract additional buyers, whose purchases further increase prices. Overconfidence bias leads investors to overestimate their ability to identify profitable investments and to underestimate risks, particularly during periods of sustained price increases when past success reinforces the belief that further gains are likely. The disposition effect—investors' tendency to sell winning investments too quickly while holding losing ones too long—can slow the price correction process once a bubble begins to deflate. Perhaps most importantly, the concept of 'limits to arbitrage,' developed by Andrei Shleifer and Robert Vishny, explains why rational investors may be unable to correct mispricings even when they recognize them. Short-selling a bubble requires borrowing securities, paying carrying costs, and maintaining positions during potentially extended periods of further price increases—risks that can bankrupt even well-informed investors before the market corrects. This insight fundamentally challenges the efficient market assumption that informed trading will always push prices toward fundamental values. Critics of behavioral finance argue that identifying biases after the fact is easier than predicting bubbles in advance, and that behavioral models have not yet produced reliable forecasting tools that outperform efficient market benchmarks.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: explain how behavioral finance accounts for market bubbles and discuss the concept's limitations
argue that all investors are irrational and should not participate in financial markets
compare stock market returns across different countries and time periods
explain the mathematical formulas used in financial modeling
propose specific regulations to prevent future market bubbles

questions.explanation: The passage covers behavioral biases, limits to arbitrage, and criticisms of behavioral finance.

questions.questionStem: The concept of 'limits to arbitrage' is significant because it explains why

options.text: rational investors can always correct mispricings immediately
informed investors may be unable to correct bubble pricing due to the costs and risks of short-selling
arbitrage has been made illegal in most financial markets
stock prices always reflect fundamental values perfectly
only institutional investors can participate in financial markets

questions.explanation: Short-selling requires 'borrowing securities, paying carrying costs, and maintaining positions during potentially extended periods of further price increases—risks that can bankrupt even well-informed investors.'

questions.questionStem: Herding behavior contributes to bubbles because it creates

options.text: a process in which rising prices attract more buyers, whose purchases further increase prices
a situation in which all investors sell simultaneously
perfectly efficient price discovery
a decrease in trading volume during price increases
a permanent state of price stability

questions.explanation: Herding 'can create self-reinforcing price increases as rising prices attract additional buyers, whose purchases further increase prices.'

questions.questionStem: Critics of behavioral finance argue that

options.text: cognitive biases do not exist in any context
identifying biases after the fact is easier than predicting bubbles in advance, and behavioral models lack reliable forecasting tools
efficient market hypothesis has been completely abandoned by all economists
behavioral finance has already produced perfect prediction tools
financial markets should not be studied by any academic discipline

questions.explanation: Critics argue 'identifying biases after the fact is easier than predicting bubbles in advance' and models haven't outperformed 'efficient market benchmarks.'

passage: Feeding a global population projected to reach nearly 10 billion by 2050 while mitigating the environmental impact of agriculture represents one of the defining challenges of the twenty-first century. Agriculture already accounts for approximately 70 percent of global freshwater withdrawals, occupies roughly half of the world's habitable land, and contributes an estimated 25 percent of greenhouse gas emissions. Simply expanding cropland to meet growing demand is not a viable option, as most of the remaining arable land consists of forests and wetlands whose conversion would release stored carbon and destroy biodiversity. Increasing productivity on existing farmland—closing the 'yield gap' between what farms currently produce and what they could produce using best available practices—offers one of the most promising pathways. In sub-Saharan Africa, where yield gaps are largest, improved access to irrigation, fertilizers, high-yielding crop varieties, and agricultural extension services could significantly increase food production without expanding farmland. Reducing food waste presents another major opportunity. The United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization estimates that approximately one-third of food produced globally is lost or wasted, amounting to 1.3 billion tons annually. In developing countries, losses occur primarily after harvest due to inadequate storage, transportation, and processing infrastructure. In wealthy nations, waste is concentrated at the consumer and retail levels, driven by cosmetic standards, overpurchasing, and confusing date labels. Addressing food waste across the supply chain could feed an additional two billion people with food already being produced. Dietary shifts, particularly reducing meat consumption in wealthy nations, could also ease pressure on agricultural systems, as animal agriculture requires significantly more land, water, and energy per calorie produced than plant-based foods.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: discuss strategies for feeding a growing global population while addressing agriculture's environmental impact
argue that all meat consumption should be immediately banned worldwide
compare agricultural productivity in different regions of the world
explain the biology of crop growth and photosynthesis
evaluate which country has the most efficient agricultural system

questions.explanation: The passage covers yield gap closure, food waste reduction, and dietary shifts as strategies.

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, simply expanding cropland is not viable because

options.text: there is no remaining land on Earth
most remaining arable land is forest or wetland whose conversion would harm the environment
farmers refuse to cultivate new land
expanding cropland would not increase food production
governments have banned all land conversion worldwide

questions.explanation: 'Most of the remaining arable land consists of forests and wetlands whose conversion would release stored carbon and destroy biodiversity.'

questions.questionStem: The passage suggests that food waste differs between developing and wealthy nations in that

options.text: developing countries waste more food than wealthy nations
developing country losses occur post-harvest while wealthy nation waste is at consumer and retail levels
neither developing nor wealthy nations experience food waste
food waste only occurs in wealthy nations
developing countries have completely eliminated food waste

questions.explanation: In developing countries losses come from 'inadequate storage, transportation, and processing' while in wealthy nations waste is at 'consumer and retail levels.'

passage: The origin and evolution of human language remains one of the most debated topics in the sciences, partly because language leaves no direct fossil record. Unlike stone tools or skeletal remains, spoken words do not preserve, forcing researchers to rely on indirect evidence from comparative anatomy, genetics, archaeology, and the study of living languages. The discovery that the FOXP2 gene, which plays a role in the fine motor control necessary for speech production, underwent significant mutations in the human lineage approximately 200,000 years ago provided one of the first genetic clues about the timing of language evolution. Anatomical evidence offers additional insights. The position of the human larynx, which sits lower in the throat than in other primates, creates a larger vocal tract that enables the production of a wider range of sounds. The size and configuration of the hypoglossal canal, which carries the nerve controlling tongue movement, also differ significantly between modern humans and earlier hominids, suggesting enhanced tongue control for articulate speech. Competing theories about how language originated fall broadly into two camps. Gradualist theories propose that language evolved incrementally from simpler communication systems, such as primate vocalizations and gestures, through natural selection acting on successive refinements. Saltationist theories, by contrast, suggest that a single genetic mutation or a small number of mutations produced a qualitative leap in cognitive capacity—specifically, the ability to combine discrete units of meaning according to recursive grammatical rules, a feature sometimes called 'merge' that appears unique to human language. Noam Chomsky has been the most prominent advocate of this position, arguing that the combinatorial complexity of human grammar could not have arisen through gradual adaptation. The debate remains unresolved, with most researchers acknowledging that both gradual and sudden processes likely played roles in the emergence of this uniquely human capacity.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: discuss the challenges and key evidence in understanding how human language evolved
argue that Noam Chomsky's theory of language is definitively correct
explain how children learn to speak their first language
compare the communication systems of different animal species
describe the anatomy of the human vocal tract in detail

questions.explanation: The passage covers evidence (genetic, anatomical) and competing theories (gradualist vs. saltationist).

questions.questionStem: Studying the evolution of language is particularly challenging because

options.text: scientists have no interest in the topic
spoken language does not leave a direct fossil record, requiring reliance on indirect evidence
language has not changed at all in human history
only one language has ever existed
genetic analysis is impossible for human populations

questions.explanation: 'Language leaves no direct fossil record,' forcing reliance on 'indirect evidence from comparative anatomy, genetics, archaeology.'

questions.questionStem: According to the passage, the saltationist theory of language evolution proposes that

options.text: language evolved very gradually over millions of years from primate calls
a small number of genetic mutations produced a qualitative leap in cognitive capacity for grammar
all animals have the same language capabilities as humans
language was invented by a single individual
language evolution occurred through cultural learning alone, not biology

questions.explanation: Saltationist theories suggest 'a single genetic mutation or a small number of mutations produced a qualitative leap in cognitive capacity.'

passage: The rapid growth of cryptocurrency markets has created an urgent need for regulatory frameworks that balance innovation with consumer protection and financial stability. Bitcoin, the first and most widely known cryptocurrency, was designed specifically to operate outside traditional financial regulation. However, as the cryptocurrency market expanded to encompass thousands of tokens with a combined market capitalization that peaked at nearly $3 trillion in 2021, the case for regulation became increasingly difficult to ignore. High-profile failures have underscored the risks of an unregulated market. The collapse of the FTX exchange in 2022, which resulted in the loss of billions of dollars in customer funds, demonstrated that cryptocurrency exchanges can pose risks similar to those of traditional financial institutions—risks that existing regulatory frameworks were designed to prevent. Regulatory approaches have varied significantly across jurisdictions. The European Union's Markets in Crypto-Assets regulation, finalized in 2023, established a comprehensive framework covering crypto asset service providers, stablecoin issuers, and market abuse prevention. The United States has taken a less unified approach, with multiple agencies—the Securities and Exchange Commission, Commodity Futures Trading Commission, and state regulators—asserting overlapping and sometimes contradictory authority over different aspects of the cryptocurrency market. China has taken the most restrictive approach, effectively banning cryptocurrency trading and mining. Industry participants argue that overly restrictive regulation could drive innovation to more permissive jurisdictions, while consumer advocates contend that the crypto industry's resistance to regulation has enabled widespread fraud, market manipulation, and investor losses. Finding the appropriate regulatory balance remains an active and contentious policy challenge.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: describe the need for cryptocurrency regulation and compare different jurisdictional approaches
argue that all cryptocurrencies should be banned immediately
explain the blockchain technology underlying cryptocurrency
compare the investment returns of different cryptocurrencies
evaluate which cryptocurrency exchange is the most secure

questions.explanation: The passage covers the need for regulation, failures like FTX, and different approaches in the EU, US, and China.

options.text: praise the cryptocurrency industry for its transparency
demonstrate that unregulated crypto exchanges can pose risks similar to traditional financial institutions
argue that all exchanges are equally secure
compare FTX with traditional stock exchanges
show that cryptocurrency investors never lose money

questions.explanation: FTX 'demonstrated that cryptocurrency exchanges can pose risks similar to those of traditional financial institutions.'

questions.questionStem: The passage characterizes the U.S. approach to cryptocurrency regulation as

a comprehensive unified framework similar to the EU's approach
completely absent of any regulatory oversight

questions.explanation: 'Multiple agencies... asserting overlapping and sometimes contradictory authority over different aspects.'

passage: The widespread deployment of renewable energy sources, particularly solar and wind power, has created an urgent need for effective energy storage solutions. Unlike fossil fuel power plants, which can generate electricity on demand, solar and wind installations produce power intermittently—only when the sun is shining or the wind is blowing. Without adequate storage, excess energy generated during peak production periods is wasted, and electricity supply during low-production periods must be supplemented by conventional power plants, limiting the share of renewables in the overall energy mix. Lithium-ion batteries currently dominate the energy storage market, benefiting from decades of development driven by the consumer electronics and electric vehicle industries. Grid-scale lithium-ion installations have grown rapidly, with global capacity exceeding 45 gigawatt-hours in 2023. However, lithium-ion technology faces limitations for long-duration storage—periods longer than four hours—due to high costs and degradation concerns. Several alternative technologies are being developed to address this gap. Pumped-storage hydropower, which stores energy by pumping water uphill to a reservoir and releasing it through turbines when electricity is needed, accounts for over 90 percent of global installed storage capacity but requires specific geographic conditions. Flow batteries, which store energy in liquid electrolytes held in external tanks, can be scaled more flexibly than lithium-ion systems and offer longer lifespans. Compressed air energy storage and gravitational energy storage using heavy weights represent additional approaches at various stages of development. Green hydrogen—produced by using renewable electricity to split water into hydrogen and oxygen—has attracted significant interest as a long-duration storage medium, though current production costs remain high and energy conversion efficiency is relatively low.

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: explain why energy storage is essential for renewable energy and survey the main storage technologies
argue that lithium-ion batteries are the only viable storage technology
compare the costs of renewable energy with those of fossil fuels
explain the physics of solar panel electricity generation
evaluate which energy storage company is the best investment

questions.explanation: The passage covers the intermittency problem, lithium-ion dominance and limits, and alternative technologies.

high costs and degradation concerns for periods longer than four hours
the fact that they have never been used for grid-scale applications
environmental regulations that prohibit their use
their requirement for extremely cold operating temperatures

questions.explanation: Lithium-ion technology 'faces limitations for long-duration storage—periods longer than four hours—due to high costs and degradation concerns.'

questions.questionStem: Green hydrogen is considered promising for energy storage despite

options.text: being identical to traditional fossil fuel hydrogen
current high production costs and relatively low energy conversion efficiency
the fact that water cannot be split into hydrogen and oxygen
being banned in most countries
requiring no electricity for production

questions.explanation: Green hydrogen has attracted interest 'though current production costs remain high and energy conversion efficiency is relatively low.'

passage: Psychologists have long studied what motivates human behavior, developing theories that have important applications in education, business, and personal development. One of the most influential frameworks distinguishes between intrinsic motivation—engaging in an activity because it is inherently interesting or enjoyable—and extrinsic motivation—engaging in an activity to obtain an external reward or avoid a punishment. Research by Edward Deci and Richard Ryan, the developers of self-determination theory, has shown that intrinsic motivation is generally associated with higher quality performance, greater creativity, and more sustained engagement than extrinsic motivation. Their theory identifies three basic psychological needs—autonomy, competence, and relatedness—that must be satisfied for intrinsic motivation to flourish. When people feel that they have control over their actions, that they are capable of meeting challenges, and that they are connected to others, they are most likely to be internally motivated. Counterintuitively, research has also demonstrated that introducing external rewards for activities that people already find intrinsically enjoyable can actually reduce their motivation—a phenomenon known as the 'overjustification effect.' In one classic study, children who were promised a reward for drawing spent less time drawing in subsequent free-play periods than children who received no reward, suggesting that the external incentive had undermined their natural interest in the activity. These findings have practical implications for management and education. Organizations that rely heavily on bonuses and performance metrics may inadvertently suppress the internal motivation that drives creative problem-solving and innovation. Schools that emphasize grades over learning may produce students who are skilled at earning marks but who lack genuine intellectual curiosity.

questions.questionStem: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.text: explaining theories of human motivation and their practical implications
arguing that all external rewards should be eliminated from workplaces
comparing motivation levels across different cultures
describing the neurological basis of motivation in the brain
evaluating which motivational theory is most scientifically accurate

questions.explanation: The passage covers intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation, self-determination theory, and practical applications.

questions.questionStem: The 'overjustification effect' occurs when

options.text: intrinsic motivation increases after receiving external rewards
external rewards for already enjoyable activities actually reduce internal motivation
people become more creative after receiving bonuses
punishment increases motivation for all tasks
people with no motivation suddenly become highly driven

questions.explanation: 'Introducing external rewards for activities that people already find intrinsically enjoyable can actually reduce their motivation.'

options.text: money, power, and fame
autonomy, competence, and relatedness
food, shelter, and safety
intelligence, creativity, and perseverance
speed, accuracy, and consistency

questions.explanation: The three needs are 'autonomy, competence, and relatedness.'

passage: The emergence of commercial space tourism—recreational spaceflight offered to paying customers—marks a new chapter in the history of human space exploration. In 2021, both Blue Origin and Virgin Galactic began offering suborbital flights to private citizens, with tickets priced in the hundreds of thousands of dollars. SpaceX has gone further, launching private citizens on orbital missions and planning lunar flyby tours. Proponents argue that space tourism will help fund the development of space technologies that benefit humanity more broadly, much as early aviation tourism helped drive the development of commercial air travel. The revenue generated by space tourism could support research in areas such as satellite communications, Earth observation, and asteroid mining. Some advocates also emphasize the 'overview effect'—the cognitive shift reported by astronauts who see Earth from space—arguing that exposing more people to this perspective could inspire greater environmental consciousness and global cooperation. However, critics raise substantial objections. The environmental impact of rocket launches—which emit significant quantities of carbon dioxide, black carbon, and other pollutants directly into the upper atmosphere—sits uneasily alongside claims of environmental benefit. A single suborbital flight produces roughly the same carbon emissions as a transatlantic commercial flight but carries only a handful of passengers. The extreme wealth required to participate in space tourism has also provoked criticism at a time of growing economic inequality; the resources devoted to recreational spaceflight, critics argue, could achieve far greater social impact if directed toward addressing poverty, disease, or climate change on Earth. Questions about liability, safety regulation, and the applicability of international space law to commercial tourism passengers also remain largely unresolved.
passageTitle: Space Tourism: Commercial and Ethical Dimensions

questions.questionStem: The primary purpose of the passage is to

options.text: discuss the emergence of space tourism, arguments in its favor, and criticisms it has attracted
argue that space tourism should be made available to everyone at low cost
explain the engineering behind commercial rocket design
compare the business strategies of Blue Origin, Virgin Galactic, and SpaceX
predict when space tourism will become affordable for average consumers

questions.explanation: The passage covers the emergence, proponents' arguments, and substantial criticisms.
